entries.title: Opening Day starts clock on White Sox GM Chris Getz to move from rebuild to results
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — Chris Getz inherited an organizational dumpster fire when he was named the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>’ general manager late in the 2023 season.</p><p>He oversaw the worst season in modern MLB history while tearing it down to the studs <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2024/09/27/121-white-sox-fall-to-playoff-bound-tigers-set-all-time-record-for-losses-in-season" target="_blank" ><u>in 2024</u></a>, and a promising wave of new talent wasn’t enough to avoid a third consecutive triple-digit-loss finish <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/09/28/shane-smith-nationals-white-sox-2025-102-losses" target="_blank" ><u>in 2025</u></a>.</p><p>So after an astounding 223 losses — 243, if you don’t want to give him a pass on those last few miserable weeks of ‘23 — plus bursts and starts of incremental progress that have frustrated a beleaguered fan base, the clock on Getz’ front-office tenure starts in earnest Thursday in Milwaukee.</p><p>“What we're doing is headed in the right direction,” Getz said Wednesday during the Sox’ final workout before the season begins. “As the season progresses, much like the second half last year, there's going to be stretches where, wow, we're really starting to be competitive on a regular basis and win series against clubs that are at the top of the division or top of the league.</p><p>“We just want to stay the course. Obviously, we'll adjust as things happen. We're going to have younger players from our minor league system that are welcomed to the big leagues to help this group. I want [fans] to enjoy the ride because we feel like there's a lot of good things happening on this roster, and that it's going to start showing up in the win column.”</p><p>While the ride has rarely been fun for fans for over the past five years, there are plenty of reasons to buy into the theme of “momentum” that the Sox’ marketing department is selling this season.</p><p>Look no farther than Opening Day starter <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/23/sox-starter-shane-smith-roughed-up-in-first-outing-of-spring" target="_blank" ><u>Shane Smith</u></a>, the Sox’ lone All-Star last year who will be carrying an even bigger chip on his shoulder than usual against the Brewers, who let him slip away to Getz in last season’s Rule 5 draft.</p><p>Then there’s the shortstop, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/11/white-sox-shortstop-colson-montgomery-will-venable-michael-jordan" target="_blank" ><u>Colson Montgomery</u></a>, whose career almost looked to be in jeopardy after a brutal start last year at Triple-A Charlotte, only to storm to the majors with flashes of superstardom in the second half.</p><p>Montgomery comprises half of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2025/07/08/white-sox-colson-montgomery-chase-meidroth-just-want-to-grow-and-bro-together" target="_blank" ><u>a Chicago baseball bromance-in-the-making</u></a> with second baseman Chase Meidroth, who plays the style of grinder-ball that’s praised on the concourse walls of Rate Field but that has been sorely lacking on the diamond itself this decade.</p><p>Getz even showed flashes of building rather than rebuilding over the offseason, bringing in Japanese slugging wunderkind <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/16/white-sox-have-to-get-munetaka-murakami-up-to-speed-after-lengthy-absence" target="_blank" ><u>Munetaka Murakami</u></a> and making a two-year, $34 million bet that his overseas power will translate against big-league pitching. The GM also bolstered last year’s heartbreaking bullpen with a legitimate back-end option in World Series-tested right-hander Seranthony Domínguez.</p><p>Perhaps most importantly, Getz has a respected partner in the clubhouse, a far cry from the cantankerous ex-manager Pedro Grifol, the misguided hire whom Getz was stuck with when he walked in the door. Manager Will Venable is as beloved by his players as he is adept at shifting focus on win totals to the process of creating a winning atmosphere.</p><p>“We want to take a set forward for sure,” Venable said on the eve of his second Opening Day as skipper. “When we really pin down the step forward we want to take, it's executing those plays, and knowing that if we're able to do that on a… more consistent basis, then we're gonna get the results that we want.”</p><p>After dealing Luis Robert Jr. for the unproven Luisangel Acuña, the only thing Getz doesn’t have are any more big trade chips — or any more excuses. He’s had over two years to address an outfield that remains full of question marks and a rotation with even more.</p><p>No one’s expecting a playoff run. No one’s ruling out a wild-card flirtation, even if it’s just a week or two of meaningful games after the All-Star break.</p><p>But looking at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/25/white-sox-opening-day-roster-korey" >this Opening Day roster</a>, no one can rule out the possibility of a fourth straight year of 100-loss futility, either.</p><p>It’ll be Getz’ mess to own, or his claim to executive fame. Either way, it’s all on him now.<br></p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Coach Billy Donovan discussed his future with the Bulls and the rumors
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA – Billy Donovan was never looking for legacy. The Bulls coach hasn’t chased down notoriety, the Hall of Fame, or a pay day.</p><p>What he made clear on Wednesday, however, was what he will be searching for when the season ends are answers.</p><p>And while Donovan never came out and said it, the answers he hears from ownership and specifically his front office will carry a lot of weight in the decisions he makes in his immediate future.</p><p>The Sun-Times reported on Tuesday that there was growing momentum around the building that Donovan could be looking to move on once the season ends, and that of course was the topic of discussion for the coach before the game with Philadelphia.</p><p>“When I got into coaching I never did it for the money piece of it and I never really did it for notoriety,” Donovan said. “I love the game, I loved competing, and I felt like outside my parents, the people that had the most influence on me in my life were the guys that coached me.</p><p>“I never really thought, ‘Hey listen, I’m going to get into coaching because I want to be in the Hall of Fame.’ I wanted to get into coaching to win and the competing part, and trying to get a group of guys how to compete, so I never looked at it from a legacy standpoint as much as the season comes to an end, how are we going to work to get into a place where we’re really competing and how do we build this out moving forward? Those are the things that we need to talk about.</p><p>“Selfishly, competitively, I want us to be in that situation where we’re playing in real legitimate, not that these (regular-season games) aren’t legitimate, but you’re in the playoffs, you’re going through a seven-game series and you’re trying to advance and move on.”</p><p>Something Donovan hasn’t had the chance to do much in his six seasons, making the postseason just once.</p><p>And while the Donovan critics point the finger in his direction, the fact is he’s been given one perennial All-Star in an aging DeMar DeRozan and arguably got some of DeRozan’s best basketball out of him.</p><p>For the most part, executive vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas gave him one legitimate playoff-caliber team and when Lonzo Ball went down with a season-ending injury the tires seemed to fall off.</p><p>Now the Bulls will be heading into a third attempted rebuild and Donovan needs to hear a plan that he can get behind.</p><p>“Whatever words you want to use we’re pivoting right now, so a lot of it is going to be me sitting down with ownership, with Arturas, to find out, ‘OK, what’s the next step?’ “ Donovan said. “Because I think the biggest thing with the moves that were made at the (trade) deadline was, ‘OK, how do we get out of the middle? And if we’re taking a step back, what do we have to do to take a step forward where we’re actually building something where, yeah, we may have to go through a plan again, but how are we going to get into a playoff?’ “</p><p>There were rumors the last 24 hours that North Carolina would be reaching out to Donovan after firing Hubert Davis, but Donovan said his focus was on the Bulls and the remaining 10 games.</p><p>And while Donovan all but dismissed a return to college a few years back, NIL and the transfer portal have changed the landscape enough where there could be an attraction.</p><p>“I’ve had times in college where you’re not in the tournament and you’re not in the mix,” Donovan added. “That’s always disappointing because you want to be in the mix of trying to win a championship. I want to be in that situation. I understand it doesn’t happen overnight and in the blink of an eye. It takes a lot of hard work and diligence; you’ve got to build and really identify the players you want to have.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Bulls suffer pain and injuries in 20-point blowout loss to Philadelphia
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA – It no longer feels like Jaden Ivey has a shrinking runway for a return this season.</p><p>It’s down to about a sidewalk.</p><p>Bulls coach Billy Donovan gave the latest update on Ivey on Wednesday, and it wasn’t great. Acquired from Detroit in the Kevin Huerter trade, Ivey was back practicing last week after a month-long knee strengthening program and of course collided with a teammate.</p><p>Before that setback, Ivey was expected to return to play at some point on this current four-city road trip. A trip that Ivey was no longer a part of.</p><p>“After he banged knees, he did have some swelling,” Donovan said. “That’s down now. He’s made some progress, but he’s not on this trip now, he was (left) behind and try to continue to work on his strength and get him stronger. Banging the knee gave him a little more discomfort.”</p><p>Not the only discomfort Donovan’s roster was going through.</p><p>While the one-sided 157-137 laugher against Philadelphia was bad enough, the Bulls also lost Nick Richards with a right elbow injury and Jalen Smith to his recurring calf issue in the first half. Not ideal for a team already playing undersized in the frontcourt, and 76ers center Joel Embiid made them pay with 35 points in 28 minutes of work.</p><p>“We’re not mathematically eliminated, but we’re pretty much eliminated,” guard Josh Giddey said of the latest loss. “I think the important thing (the rest of the season) is we try and play the right way.”</p><p>Basically, the opposite of what they did against Philadelphia, especially in a third quarter in which they allowed 51 points.</p><p>“It was disappointing,” Donovan said. “Even before Jalen got hurt, Nick got hurt, we didn’t start off the game shooting the ball well.”</p><p>Donovan thought that poor start bled into all aspects of the game and there was no recovery.</p><p>Meanwhile, the Bulls (29-43) at least have some immediate clarity with Smith and Richards. Smith has one more year left on his deal, while Richards is a free agent. The challenge of the Ivey situation is he’s a restricted free agent. The Bulls would like to see if he could get back to the explosive player he was coming out of Purdue before committing, but that might not happen.</p><p>Ideally, Ivey stays in Chicago and works with the medical team after the season, but every player is different.</p><p>“There’s no question that when it comes to Jaden our medical people would be absolutely open minded about him staying (in Chicago), working with him and getting him stronger, but ultimately once the season ends, I’m sure there will be some collaboration with our medical guys, any second opinions with doctors, coaches, to figure out what’s the best avenue to work on his strength and get him back on the court playing,” Donovan said.</p><p></p><p><b>Mr. Smith’s future</b></p><p></p><p>Donovan did say after the game that because Smith continues to be plagued by the calf injury, there has to be a discussion on whether or not they should shut him down for the remainder of the season.</p><p>Smith will make $9.4 million next season and then could become a free agent.</p><p></p><p><b>Say what?</b></p><p></p><p>The 157 points by Philadelphia was the most points they scored in a game since the 1970 season. The Bulls trailed by as much as 39 and allowed 122 points after three quarters.</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Man, 65, shot in North Lawndale
entries.summary: <p>A 65-year-old man was hospitalized after being shot in North Lawndale Wednesday night, according to Chicago police.</p><p>The man was in the 1300 block of South Kedzie Avenue at 5:54 p.m. when he was shot in the neck, police said in a statement. He was taken to Mt. Sinai Hospital in serious condition, police said.</p><p>No one is in custody, police said.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Cold front will bring storms Thursday, potential for more tornadoes
entries.summary: <p>A powerful cold front could bring severe thunderstorms to northern Illinois after temperatures reach into the 70s Thursday morning, forecasters say.</p><p>Scattered storms are expected hit the Chicago area around 4 or 5 p.m., according to National Weather Service meteorologist Mark Ratzer. Blustery winds may hit the lakefront, and some storms could persist into the evening.</p><p>Storms may reach father south, near Kankakee, in the late afternoon. That's where there is potential for supercells to develop again with a tornado, hail and wind threat.</p><p>“The setup for tomorrow is somewhat similar to what we had March 10, but not quite as conducive to track prolific tornadoes,” Ratzer said. “Though there certainly is a tornado threat.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Here's a timing graphic illustrating where we expect the highest coverage of storms and when. <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/ILwx?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#ILwx</a> <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/INwx?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#INwx</a><br>(🧵2/3) <a href="https://t.co/NGrUS2ccOt">pic.twitter.com/NGrUS2ccOt</a></p>— NWS Chicago (@NWSChicago) <a href="https://twitter.com/NWSChicago/status/2036887065137164627?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 25, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Southern areas could also see temperatures rise to the mid or upper 80s Thursday morning ahead of the storms, he said. The Chicago area will likely reach the upper 50s and lower 60s. The western suburbs may hit the low 70s. </p><p>Temperatures may drop as the cold front rolls in further north around 4 p.m., dropping the lake front to the mid 40s.</p><p>About two weeks ago, a total of 10 tornadoes that ravaged the region, including an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/12/kankakee-county-tornado-damage-amount-state-relief-funds" target="_blank" ><u>EF-3 that killed at least three</u></a> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/11/2-killed-after-storms-bring-at-least-4-twisters-possible-record-setting-hail-in-illinois-and-indiana" target="_blank" ><u>people</u></a> outside Kankakee and produced <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/weather/2026/03/12/hail-record-size-chicago-illinois-storm-climate-change" target="_blank" ><u>record-breaking hail</u></a>.</p><p>“It’s springtime. It’s severe weather season,” Ratzer said. “Have a way to receive warnings and get to safety.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Here are our Key Messages for Thursday (3/26). <a href="https://t.co/lQSCAxkNrZ">pic.twitter.com/lQSCAxkNrZ</a></p>— NWS Storm Prediction Center (@NWSSPC) <a href="https://twitter.com/NWSSPC/status/2036866114626437270?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 25, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Northwestern hires Princeton's Carla Berube as women's basketball coach
entries.summary: <p>Northwestern hired former UConn star and Princeton coach Carla Berube as its women's basketball coach on Wednesday.</p><p>Berube replaces the retired Joe McKeown and will be tasked with turning around a program that hasn't won more than nine games in a season since 2021-22.</p><p>Berube, who helped UConn win its first NCAA championship in 1995 under Hall of Fame coach Geno Auriemma, led Princeton to a 147-29 record and five NCAA Tournament appearances in six seasons. The Tigers went 26-4 overall and 12-2 in Ivy League play this season, winning the conference's regular-season and tournament championships before losing to Oklahoma State in the first round of March Madness.</p><p>Berube coached All-Americans Abby Meyers and Kaitlyn Chen, both of whom transferred to bigger programs before being selected in the WNBA Draft. Bella Alarie also made it to the WNBA after playing four seasons at Princeton.</p><p>"This is a place where academic and athletic excellence go hand in hand, and I'm excited to build a championship culture that reflects that standard," Berube said in a statement. "Competing in the Big Ten Conference requires toughness, discipline, and a relentless commitment to growth. We are going to embrace that challenge and build a team that competes with pride and goes to battle for one another every night."</p><p>Northwestern reached the NCAA Tournament in 2015 and again in 2021, when it advanced for the first time in 28 years. But the Wildcats have struggled since.</p><p>McKeown had a 785-453 record with 17 NCAA Tournament appearances in 40 years at Northwestern, George Washington and New Mexico State. He was the Wildcats' winningest coach, leading them to a 276-279 record in 18 seasons.</p><p>Berube is the first major hire for athletic director Mark Jackson, who took the job in 2024. She led Division III Tufts to a 384-96 record over 17 seasons and is the program's leader in wins.</p><p>Berube scored 1,381 points for UConn from 1993 to 1997. The Huskies went 132-8, with the 1994-95 team going 35-0 to capture the program's first of its 12 national titles.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

Or some of you might be attracted to someone unusual or different. Meanwhile, you might choose to work alone or behind the scenes to negotiate financial matters or solidify support for yourself and others.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a popular day for you. Accept the responsibility that might be thrust on you by groups and organizations because your ability to deal with others is excellent today. You can encourage friends, partners and members of the public to help you.</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You make a great impression on everyone today because they see you as positive, energetic and generous. This is why others will follow your lead. Use this power you have over others to do something good. In fact, whatever way you help others today will come back to you increased.</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> If you can travel today, this will thrill you. If you can't travel, perhaps you can make travel plans. Alternatively, you can travel through film, conversation with someone and study. Grab every opportunity to express your creativity in the arts, the entertainment world, sports and working with children.</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> It looks like you have the practical and financial support to make improvements at home today. Certainly, you will want to do something because increased chaos and activity on the home front are a challenge. (Is this because visiting relatives are camped on your doorstep?)</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an excellent day to reach out to others or work in conjunction with spouses, partners and close friends because any kind of group energy will benefit you (as well as everyone else). Let enthusiasm and generosity inspire others and guide your decisions.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-4d0000" name="html-embed-module-4d0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>An <b>average</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Work-related travel is likely for many of you today. This is also a solid day to discuss money, earnings and cash flow. Certainly, you have strong opinions about what is owed to you, and you intend to get it. With the sun and Venus in your house of communications, you will walk your talk!</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a fun-loving, playful day. Accept invitations to schmooze and party, as well as enjoy sports events and fun activities with kids. Exercise and a chance to burn some energy will invigorate you. Accept any opportunity that comes your way to express your creative talents.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Comedian, actor and writer Martin Short (1950) shares your birthday today. You are easygoing and quick-thinking. People admire your straightforwardness, clear-sightedness and practicality. Lucky you! Prepare for a playful, fun-loving year. Enjoy socializing with others. Your creativity and desire for life are at a pinnacle. Let your guard down and loosen up a bit. Old friends might reappear in your world. </p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: Cubs have high expectations, but they realize it will be a long haul
entries.summary: <p>The wait is over. The preparations have been made. The Cubs’ table is ready.</p><p>It’s time to play ball.</p><p>“It’s here,” third baseman Alex Bregman said.</p><p>It’s going to be an Opening Day that will get the juices flowing for Bregman, 32, who signed a five-year, $175 million contract as a free agent in the offseason. This will be his 11th season, and the gleam in his eye still suggests a sincerity about everything that Opening Day at Wrigley Field will be when the Cubs host the Nationals at 1:20 p.m. Thursday.</p><p>“The excitement is at an all-time high for me,” Bregman said. “First year with the Cubs, experiencing Opening Day at Wrigley is going to be incredible.”</p><p>Bregman has played before raucous crowds in high-stakes games, including the World Baseball Classic two weeks ago.</p><p>He has played 10 seasons, all but one in Houston, where he played in four World Series, winning two. And he’s going to be nervous.</p><p>“Opening Day jitters? Oh, yeah,” Bregman said. “When you lose that, it’s time to hang ’em up. -Every Opening Day, there are nerves and excitement. This being a new chapter in my career adds excitement to it, as well.”</p><p>As first impressions with a new team go, Bregman has played to strong reviews this spring for his attention to detail, preparation and willingness to share information with teammates. The intangibles have value, but the kind of production he showed during camp — a team-high five home runs, four doubles, two strikeouts and a 1.869 OPS in 21 at-bats over nine games — would have more of an impact on winning than anything.</p><p>“It was a good spring training,” he said. “Guys are focused, motivated and ready to compete. We have a good ballclub capable of making a deep run in October, but we know the season doesn’t start in October. We’ve got a lot of work ahead of us to take care of.”</p><p>Everyone from manager Craig Counsell to the every-day players and pitchers has dwelled on the one-game-at-a-time approach. When there are 162 to play before the postseason, it’s the only way to survive and succeed.</p><p>“There are these expectations and all these things,” right-hander Cade Horton said, “but what this team is good at is focusing on the day-to-day process. Coming in and getting work, going about it the right way. Hopefully, that will take us to where we need to go.”</p><p>The Cubs are favored to win the National League Central, but doing so would fall short of their goals.</p><p>“The expectations are high, but that’s seven months from now,” left fielder Ian Happ said. “The challenge is, it’s day to day, week to week. That’s where we want to be in seven months, but we get there by playing really good baseball on Thursday. And then doing it again and doing it again.”</p><p>Conversations in camp were worthwhile, setting the tone for the 162-game marathon.</p><p>“We’re talking about little things that can help us win ballgames,” catcher Carson Kelly said. “This group in particular has a very good mix of veterans and younger guys, a lot of guys who are back from last year who know what it takes and have been to and won the World Series. Having those experiences helps everyone get better, and to know what it takes to win a World Series.”</p><p>Bregman said the attention to detail and the focus were evident in camp.</p><p>“There’s also a good togetherness that’s important in building a playoff team,” he said.</p><p>The mission starts Opening Day, and Happ entered his 10th season with the Cubs at a place where it never gets old.</p><p>“Every Opening Day is special, and when you get to have it at Wrigley Field, the fans, the experience, it’s unbelievable,” Happ said. “There will be 40,000 excited people there. Fans have waited all winter. To do it in front of those fans is really special.”<br></p>
entries.author: Daryl Van Schouwen

entries.title: Man fatally shot during argument in Woodlawn
entries.summary: <p>Police are looking for two assailants inside a white Mercedes who fatally shot a man in another car during an argument Wednesday morning in the Woodlawn neighborhood. </p><p>The 29-year-old victim was inside his vehicle in the 6200 block of South Drexel Avenue around 11:30 a.m. Wednesday two people inside a white Mercedes pulled up and began arguing with him, police said. </p><p>One of the occupants in the Mercedes shot the man in the chest and he was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Man found dead after flames doused in Roseland fire
entries.summary: <p>A 65-year-old man was found dead inside a home that erupted in flames early Thursday in Roseland.</p><p>The blaze erupted around 3 a.m. at a home in the 9600 block of South LaSalle Street and after firefighters extinguished the flames, they found the victim inside the home, unresponsive, police said. </p><p>He was was pronounced dead at the scene, police said. </p><p>No additional injuries or displacements were reported, officials said. </p><p>Fire officials were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: George Gervin on Bears' Caleb Williams: 'Iceman' nickname already taken
entries.summary: <p>George Gervin isn’t looking for a beef. But when he found out that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears" target="_blank" >Bears</a> quarterback Caleb Williams applied to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/03/19/bears-caleb-williams-moves-trademark-iceman-nickname-patent-office-nfl" target="_blank" >trademark the “Iceman” nickname</a>, the former San Antonio Spurs star decided to fight for the moniker he believes is his.</p><p></p><p>“I’ve got nothing but respect for [Williams],” Gervin told the Sun-Times on Wednesday. “He’s already proved greatness and his potential upside is great. Like an ‘Iceman.’ But that name is taken. …</p><p></p><p>“All I’m saying is: Young fella, we've already got one ‘Iceman.’”</p><p></p><p>Gervin, who won scoring titles in four different pro seasons, was inducted into the Naismith Basketball Hall of Fame in 1996 after a career that included 11 years with the Spurs as well as stops with the Virginia Squires and, for one season, the Bulls. He once averaged 29.5 points per game at Eastern Michigan and is best-known for his finger roll shot.</p><p></p><p>Caleb Williams Holding, Inc., submitted four trademark applications on March 16 related to “Iceman” — for the name, a logo and two silhouettes that mimic Williams’ pose as he rolled left, jumped and completed a pass to Rome Odunze in the Bears’ playoff win against the Packers. Goods and services related to the name include, among other things, athletic bags, water bottles, sweatshirts, T-shirts, jerseys, hats, sports equipment, eyewear and downloadable posters and trading cards.</p><p></p><p>On March 20, four days after Williams’ application was filed, Gervin Interests LLC filed trademark applications for both “Iceman” and “Iceman 44,” referencing Gervin’s jersey number. Related goods and services include entertainment services and personal appearances by Gervin in addition to T-shirts, hats, shirts, wristbands, sweatshirts and other pieces of apparel. The application notes that Gervin’s “Iceman” nickname was first used by him in commerce in 1979.</p><p></p><p>Jerald Barisano, president/CEO of Gervin Global Management, said he wrongly believed Gervin’s nickname was already trademarked, owing the confusion to the death of a business associate.</p><p></p><p>It will be months before the applications are processed; the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office is currently reviewing applications from early November. Barisano said he and Gervin plan to contest the trademark if it’s granted to Williams.</p><p></p><p>“We are hoping the inspectors will do the right thing,” Barisano said. “All they’ve got to do, is, do one Google search and they’ll see hundreds and hundreds of articles on the ‘Iceman,’ George Gervin.”</p><p></p><p>Williams was given the nickname last season for having ice in his veins late in games. His celebration — rubbing his opposite biceps with his hands, as if he were cold — came from Chelsea soccer player Cole Palmer, who does something similar.</p><p></p><p>Gervin, who said he was given the nickname in 1973, once met another “Iceman,” Chicago soul singer and eventual Cook County commissioner Jerry Butler. Their nicknames, he said, didn’t cause confusion because they were famous for different reasons.</p><p></p><p>Like Gervin, though, Williams is an athlete.</p><p></p><p>“I’m really the ‘Iceman’ in sports,” Gervin said.<br></p>
entries.author: Patrick Finley

entries.title: Dear Abby: Aunt's passing is followed by a bitter falling-out
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My favorite aunt was dying. Her daughter is my favorite cousin, so I texted her almost every day with encouragement. When the doctor said the end was near, I backed off messaging to give the family space to themselves.</p><p>Thirteen days later, I received a text with a photograph of my aunt's grave. Ensuing texts and phone calls indicated that my cousin's older brother had grandstanded and taken over. Questions about her service and burial went unanswered. I expressed dismay; our family had been the first to call about almost everything.</p><p>I have read a great deal about forgiveness — when to offer it, and when to forgive the messenger but not the action. That is the stance I have taken. My once-beloved cousin responds as if all is well between us, though she has asked twice if I would rather she stop communicating, given that I rarely respond. Twice I have said, "That is up to you."</p><p>Admittedly, I would love some closure on this, one way or the other. In my youth, I would have forgiven it all, probably even apologized for her "pain" while subjugating my own. I am not in that frame of mind now. Do I forgive? Forget? Move on? — <i>BEWILDERED IN FLORIDA</i></p><p><b>DEAR BEWILDERED:</b> What happened was not your cousin's fault; the fault lies with her grandstanding brother. Do not allow this to interfere with your formerly loving relationship with her. By all means, forgive and move on. Also, put aside your passive-aggressive attitude. When people are grieving a death, even one that is expected, they are usually distracted, in pain and not thinking about how their actions affect others.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I have a 42-year-old son, "Wade," who has anxiety, depression and a wild temper. He receives minimal, below-poverty-level disability payments. Wade's father and I give him money when he says he's hungry and out of food. (We are both retired.) Wade claims his lack of money is "the system's" fault.</p><p>Wade also imagines he can earn a living in the music industry. I am beyond tired of this. I'm a retired teacher. How can I help our son to become independent? Wade says our family is "broken" and doesn't work "as a team." I have my own bills to pay. — <i>OVER HIM IN TEXAS</i></p><p><b>DEAR OVER HIM:</b> If Wade defines "working as a team" to mean he can hit you up for money any time he wants, I beg to differ. If he were meant to be a success in the youth-oriented music business, his career would have been in full swing by the time he was 30.</p><p>Encourage Wade to find a way to supplement his disability payments so his cupboard won't be bare at the end of the month, and let him know firmly that you won't be giving him more handouts. Your family isn't broken; the only thing that's broke is your son.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>To order "How to Write Letters for All Occasions," send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby — Letter Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Like Trump, DHS' Mullin will trash Republicans who don't toe the party line
entries.summary: <p>Some Supreme Court justices are so eager to demonstrate their independence, President Donald Trump <a class="Link" href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116236559151421767" target="_blank" >complained</a> last week, that they "openly disrespect the Presidents who nominate them." A few days later, Sen. Markwayne Mullin, R–Okla., who was <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2026/03/23/us/politics/mullins-confirmation-throwback-senate.html" target="_blank" >confirmed</a> this week as secretary of homeland security, explained why he had <a class="Link" href="https://www.wral.com/news/ap/8eaf5-the-latest-trump-fires-homeland-security-secretary-noem-amid-criticism-over-immigration-enforcement/" target="_blank" >called</a> Sen. Rand Paul, R–Ky., "a freaking snake": It "seems like you fight Republicans more than you work with us," Mullin <a class="Link" href="https://thehill.com/homenews/senate/5789478-rand-paul-markwayne-mullin-dhs/" target="_blank" >told</a> Paul.</p><p>Trump and Mullin take it for granted that public officials should be prepared to sacrifice their principles on the altar of party loyalty. But those officials, whether they serve on the Supreme Court or in the U.S. Senate, have higher duties that may require them to take stands that invite misguided rebukes like these.</p><p>The specific targets of Trump's ire were Justices Neil Gorsuch and Amy Coney Barrett, who last month had the temerity to <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/02/20/the-supreme-court-just-struck-down-trumps-emergency-tariffs/" target="_blank" >reject</a> his claim of essentially unlimited authority to impose "emergency" tariffs. In Trump's view, their defiance of his will was unforgivable because he is the guy who gave them their jobs.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-3a0000" name="module-3a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>Contrary to Trump's take, the tariff case, which <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/02/25/the-scotus-tariff-decision-vindicates-the-rule-of-law-and-the-separation-of-powers/" target="_blank" >hinged</a> on statutory interpretation informed by the separation of powers, was not a test of personal or party loyalty. If Gorsuch and Barrett had automatically agreed with the president who appointed them, they would have <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/03/17/kavanaugh-should-be-embarrassed-by-trumps-praise-for-his-tariffs-dissent/" target="_blank" >forsaken</a> their obligation to reach conclusions based on their honest assessment of the facts and the law.</p><p>Trump is not oblivious to that obligation. In fact, he <a class="Link" href="https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116236559151421767" target="_blank" >faulted</a> "the Democrats on the Court" for ignoring it, complaining that they "always 'stick together,' no matter how strong a case is put before them." Trump <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/03/20/judges-and-justices-should-be-independent-trump-says-as-long-as-they-side-with-him/" target="_blank" >thinks</a> justices should be independent, as long as they side with him.</p><p>Mullin seems similarly dismayed by Paul's periodic failures to toe the party line. But like Gorsuch and Barrett, Paul has what he takes to be good reasons for antagonizing the president and <a class="Link" href="https://www.ms.now/opinion/markwayne-mullin-cabinet-project-47-newsletter" target="_blank" >lapdog loyalists</a> like Mullin.</p><p>Paul, for instance, <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2025/10/08/the-constitution-does-not-allow-the-president-to-unilaterally-blow-suspected-drug-smugglers-to-smithereens/" target="_blank" >objects</a> to Trump's policy of <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2025/12/10/trumps-word-games-cant-conceal-the-murderous-reality-of-his-anti-drug-strategy/" target="_blank" >summarily executing</a> suspected cocaine smugglers without statutory authorization or any semblance of due process. Mullin, by contrast, <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4M9ox2_dL3o" target="_blank" >has</a> "zero problems with sending them to the bottom of the ocean."</p><p>Mullin likewise seemed untroubled when immigration agents <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/01/25/the-trump-administration-is-lying-about-gun-rights-and-the-death-of-alex-pretti/" target="_blank" >fatally shot</a> Minneapolis protester Alex Pretti on Jan. 24, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/Acyn/status/2015245390082343176" target="_blank" >saying</a> they had defended themselves against a "deranged individual" who "came in to cause max damage." Paul, by contrast, called for an independent investigation and <a class="Link" href="https://www.c-span.org/clip/news-conference/senator-rand-paul-calls-for-independent-investigation-into-alex-pretti-killing/5191081" target="_blank" >questioned</a> the initial account of the shooting, noting that it was <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2026/01/25/dhs-again-promises-a-thorough-investigation-of-a-fatal-shooting-after-prejudging-the-outcome/" target="_blank" >immediately contradicted</a> by video evidence.</p><p>Paul <a class="Link" href="https://www.foxnews.com/opinion/sen-rand-paul-america-war-but-americans-didnt-vote" target="_blank" >thinks</a> Trump should have sought congressional approval for his war with Iran, since the Constitution gives the legislative branch the sole authority to "declare war." Mullin, by contrast, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/Acyn/status/2028952215290036261" target="_blank" >can’t even seem to decide</a> whether the extensive U.S. attacks on Iran qualify as "war."</p><p>Mullin played similar word games when he parroted Trump's defense of the steep, wide-ranging, ever-changing tariffs that the Supreme Court later ruled illegal. "This isn't a trade war," Mullin <a class="Link" href="https://www.politico.com/news/2025/04/06/mullin-coons-trump-tariffs-message-00274485" target="_blank" >said</a> on Fox News last year. "This is balancing our economy with countries that have taken advantage of us."</p><p>Here, too, Paul takes a decidedly different view. The argument that "we're being ripped off" is "a fallacy," he noted in an <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QzTQd_RR1jc" target="_blank" >interview</a> with Reason's Nick Gillespie last November, because voluntary trade always entails mutual benefits.</p><p>"The farm state senators will all privately tell you, 'We are [for] free trade, and this is killing our farmers,'" Paul said. "But they will not stand up, and they will not vote with me to end the emergencies" that Trump declared to justify his tariffs.</p><p>That reticence, Paul said, reflected a broader problem. "What Founding Father would ever have imagined a Congress with no ambition?" he asked.</p><p>Most Republican legislators, Paul complained, are "feckless," "completely without principles," "afraid of their own shadow," "afraid of their own president," and "unable to even squeak." You can start to see why Mullin, who fits that description pretty well, was so mad at Paul.</p><p><i>Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-940000" name="module-940000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jacob Sullum

entries.title: Hegseth, Bondi and Gabbard may soon get the boot by 'blame-shifting' boss
entries.summary: <p>If I were Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, I might start packing up my office at the Pentagon.</p><p>While President Donald Trump is boasting about the so-called success of a war with Iran that has no clear mission nor end in sight, Americans are souring on it. Big time.</p><p>New polling from <a class="Link" href="https://www.cbsnews.com/news/opinion-poll-iran-war-regime-2028-03-22/" target="_blank" >CBS News/YouGov</a> shows an increasingly bearish public when it comes to the war, with 57% saying it’s going somewhat or very badly. Sixty-three percent believe the war will make the U.S. economy weaker in the short term, while a whopping 92% say the most important thing the U.S. should do with regard to Iran right now is “end the conflict as quickly as possible.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-df0000" name="module-df0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>Hardly a rousing endorsement. We know Trump closely monitors the polls, which is perhaps why he said what he did on Monday while at a roundtable in Memphis.</p><p>While discussing crime, Trump oddly <a class="Link" href="https://www.mediaite.com/media/tv/trump-goes-out-of-his-way-to-say-hegseth-was-the-first-to-advocate-iran-war-you-said-lets-do-it/" target="_blank" >detoured</a> to brag about his decision to go to war with Iran and then pointed to Hegseth, who was in the room, and said: “And Pete, I think you were the first one to speak up. And you said, ‘Let’s do it, because you can’t let them have a nuclear weapon.’ ”</p><p>Now, any other time and any other president and you’d call this a compliment. But seasoned Trump-watchers’ eyebrows raised at the subtle but discernible sign of what may be to come.</p><p>It would be, after all, a very Trumpian and convenient blame-shift to make the war all Pete’s idea when it’s slumping in the polls, and it doesn’t help Hegseth that <a class="Link" href="https://www.tarapalmeri.com/p/exclusive-theres-zero-confidence" target="_blank" >reports</a> are swirling around D.C. that Republicans on the Hill and inside the administration have “zero confidence” in him.</p><p>As former Department of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem knows all too well, Trump only loves you until he doesn’t. When U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement raids in Minneapolis turned deadly and the sordid headlines around her spending and personal life got too unbearable, Trump dumped the glammed-up grifter with an unceremonious thanks and a move to a <a class="Link" href="https://www.foxnews.com/politics/noem-thanks-trump-new-shield-americas-special-envoy-role-after-dhs-ouster" target="_blank" >made-up new post</a>.</p><p>Former Kennedy Center head Ric Grenell had also been one of Trump’s longtime favorites, but he too lost his job this month after overseeing plummeting ticket sales, a mass exodus of artists, and scathing headlines. And that’s after he put Trump’s name on the building!</p><p>A couple other Trump cabinet members may want to prepare for early exits, while we’re at it.</p><p>Attorney General Pam Bondi’s Department of Justice has been rightfully dragged through the mud for its incompetence and political hackery, overseeing multiple failed attempts at indicting Trump’s political opponents, an obvious cover-up of the Epstein files, and losing so much talent it now has to recruit prosecutors straight out of law school. Maybe that’s why Bondi recently hung the North Korea-coded banner of Trump on DOJ headquarters.</p><p>Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard might not be long for this world either. She’s been excluded from high-level meetings at the White House, so much so that the joke <a class="Link" href="https://katv.com/news/nation-world/tulsi-gabbard-excluded-from-the-plan-to-oust-maduro-report-venezuela-donald-trump-director-of-national-intelligence-civil-war" target="_blank" >among aides</a> is that DNI stands for “Do Not Invite.” Even Trump has <a class="Link" href="https://www.cnn.com/2025/06/17/politics/video/trump-gabbard-iran-nuclear-weapons-collins-digvid" target="_blank" >publicly dismissed</a> her advice.</p><p>If you’re wondering why these folks continue to work for a guy who humiliates them, sidelines them, and then dumps them, that’s easy — they have no principles, integrity or self-respect! And when you’re solely motivated by naked power grabs and money, you can put up with a lot.</p><p>Only time will tell if Hegseth, Bondi and Gabbard can survive what will only become worse and worse headlines. But in the meantime, Pete might want to put a Trump banner up on the Pentagon — just in case.</p><p><i>S.E. Cupp is the host of “S.E. Cupp Unfiltered” on CNN.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-7a0000" name="module-7a0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: S. E. Cupp

entries.title: Work starts on the $1.2 million restoration of landmark Pullman church's troubled but iconic bell tower
entries.summary: <p>The city-funded $1.2 million restoration of the crumbling bell tower of Pullman's historic 144-year-old Greenstone United Methodist Church is now set to begin after a five-year delay.</p><p>A team from Berglund Construction began surrounding the landmark church's 92-foot tower with scaffolding and swing stages this month.</p><p>The equipment will allow workers to get close enough to document, remove and restore the building's distinctive, but weathered, green serpentine stone cladding.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a10000" name="image-a10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f89c15/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fae%2F8b2652b6402d814e9397e4edf137%2Ftower-260327-13.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="View from across the street of Greenstone United Methodist Church, with the west side covered with scaffolding." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f89c15/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fae%2F8b2652b6402d814e9397e4edf137%2Ftower-260327-13.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3dd1cfa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fae%2F8b2652b6402d814e9397e4edf137%2Ftower-260327-13.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f89c15/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+324/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa7%2Fae%2F8b2652b6402d814e9397e4edf137%2Ftower-260327-13.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The 92-foot tower is clad with stone mined from Pennsylvania.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The project is the first substantial exterior repair the building has seen since its 1882 construction.</p><p>"I'm just so thrilled to have it kicking off," Greenstone's pastor, the Rev. Luther Mason, said. "It's been a long time."</p><p>The church, at 11211 S. St. Lawrence Ave., in the Pullman National Historical Park, was awarded a $1 million grant to restore the tower in 2021, through the Chicago Department of Planning and Development's Adopt-a-Landmark program.</p><p>Pullman Ald. Anthony Beale (9th) and Ald. Ray Lopez (15th) tabled the City Council's approval of the funds over Beale's concerns of the congregation's financial viability. But the cash was finally <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/architecture-design/2025/05/09/greenstone-united-methodist-church-pullman-restoration-bell-tower" >released last year</a> with a $200,000 bump-up.</p><p>The work will involve the painstaking removal of all of the tower's weathered stone, down to the structure's brick substrate, architect Nicole Declet, senior associate at the engineering firm Wiss Janney Elstner, said.</p><p>Under an agreement with the city, 25% of the original serpentine will be returned to the tower. The rest will be replaced with cast stone that will be designed to resemble the original cladding, Declet said.</p><p>"If we find something that's still sound, then perhaps that can be installed near the base of the tower, [closer to public view] or on other areas of the building," she said.</p><p>The city's landmarks officials and the National Park Service will watch over the restoration process.</p><p>"Once you provide that you have a good alternative material you can replace [the original material] with, and you provide the color mock-ups and the material properties, they're usually amenable to accepting a substitute material," Declet said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-780000" name="image-780000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a60cc0f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/666x374+104+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb1%2F77e13faf47a28580a99058c135a8%2Fpullman-cst-02xx14-047.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/495a9a9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/666x374+104+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb1%2F77e13faf47a28580a99058c135a8%2Fpullman-cst-02xx14-047.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15cb2ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/666x374+104+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb1%2F77e13faf47a28580a99058c135a8%2Fpullman-cst-02xx14-047.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Historic, black-and-white photo of Greenstone United Methodist Church and the then-town of Pullman, taken from the top of the Pullman Arcade Building, now demolished." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15cb2ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/666x374+104+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb1%2F77e13faf47a28580a99058c135a8%2Fpullman-cst-02xx14-047.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5214cf9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/666x374+104+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb1%2F77e13faf47a28580a99058c135a8%2Fpullman-cst-02xx14-047.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/15cb2ae/2147483647/strip/true/crop/666x374+104+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fb1%2F77e13faf47a28580a99058c135a8%2Fpullman-cst-02xx14-047.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>An historic photo of Greenstone United Methodist Church and the then-town of Pullman, taken from the top of the Pullman Arcade Building, now demolished.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times Media</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"I can't wait to see what this new stone looks like," Mason said. "People will say, 'Hey, this is what this place looked like in 1882 when we didn't have color pictures of it,' you know?"</p><p>Greenstone is the work of Solon Beman, the architect railroad car manufacturer George Pullman hired to design the entirety of what was then a factory town outside Chicago's southern border on the west banks of Lake Calumet.</p><p>Most of the buildings and employee Beman designed were built of red brick made from the clay around Lake Calumet. </p><p>But Greenstone's skin had a longer trip. The cladding came from a now-defunct quarry in Pennsylvania. It's Pullman's only green building.</p><p>"Serpentine — it's not a great material for exterior application," Declet said. "It's very porous; it's susceptible to freeze/thaw cycles."</p><p>The restoration begins just after Gov. JB Pritzker's February announcement that Pullman's 145-year-old vacant Hotel Florence, 11111 S. Forrestville Ave., its annex and a former Pullman factory building to the north will be the subject of a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/02/24/gov-jb-pritzker-100-million-redevelopment-pullman-hotel-florence" target="_blank" >$100 million revitalization effort.</a></p><p>Declet said the tower restoration could be completed around December.</p><p>"I think this will be a good sign for the community," Ed Torrez, president of Arda, a restoration architecture firm that is working with Greenstone. "Pullman's just had so much going on — of positive progress, with the [creation of the] national park and the state is going to be doing the hotel. And now we have this."</p>
entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: Congo Square ensemble members say in letter that the prominent Black theater was quietly dissolved
entries.summary: <p>Congo Square Theatre Company, a prominent Black ensemble theater group that has produced work in Chicago for a quarter century, has quietly been dissolved by its board of directors, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/photo/?fbid=1624387243027002&set=pcb.1624387346360325" target="_blank" ><u>an open letter posted on Facebook</u></a> Sunday by the ensemble.</p><p>Ensemble members confirmed the authenticity of the letter to WBEZ/Sun-Times and said they were prompted to write it, in part, after learning through lawyers that $240,000 of company money allegedly had gone to another local theater company: Victory Gardens. That alleged donation is at the center of a breach of trust lawsuit filed in December by ensemble members against members of the board in Cook County Circuit Court.</p><p>“We were never notified,” said Monifa Days, co-founding ensemble member. “Our lawyers were never notified. How we found out was we would just do searches in the state's database about where our status was. Because you have to file as a nonprofit and that's how we found out.”</p><p>As of Wednesday, the Congo Square Theatre website was down.</p><p>Congo Square’s ensemble company, co-founded in 1999 by Derrick Sanders, Reginald Nelson, and others, has been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/theater/2024/12/19/congo-square-theatre-ensemble-seeks-to-oust-board-chair" target="_blank" ><u>embroiled in disputes</u></a> with its board since 2024. That year, both Executive Director Charlique C. Rolle and Artistic Director Ericka Ratcliff stepped down from their respective positions.</p><p>In the open letter published on Facebook this week, members of the Congo Square Ensemble laid bare what they describe as a contentious two-year period that led to a pause in production and Ratcliff’s resignation, which they write happened under “significant pressure” from board chair Dawn Frances Reese.</p><p>Reese could not be reached for comment. Victory Gardens has not responded to emails for comment.</p><p>The ensemble letter and the lawsuit say that the artists’ group attempted to reconcile with the board, but failed. Requests for neutral moderation were declined, the letter and lawsuit said. That led to a year-long<b> </b>stalemate, during which the ensemble refused to program shows. Without input from the ensemble, the board moved forward with hiring an interim executive director, Charles A. Montorio-Archer, and began interviewing candidates for artistic director.</p><p>This is not the first time the artistic members of a Chicago theater have gotten out of sync with their board. The award-winning company <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2025/06/26/the-color-purple-goodman-theatre-lili-anne-brown-director" target="_blank" ><u>Bailiwick Chicago</u></a> shuttered in 2015 due to what founder Lili-Anne Brown described as “a power struggle between her and the board over artistic direction.” The Tony Award-winning Victory Gardens Theatre splintered during the COVID-19 pandemic due to protracted disagreements between its company members and its board of directors. That dispute garnered <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2022/08/01/theater/victory-gardens-theater-chicago.html" target="_blank" ><u>national attention</u></a><u>.</u></p><p>The Congo Square ensemble found out that the company might be dissolved, they reached out to their own legal team. They learned through lawyers that the company still had about $800,000. While most of the funds had been returned to major donors, $240,000 was given to Victory Gardens Theatre, according to the lawsuit.</p><p>Moving forward, the Congo Square Ensemble is now working to form a new nonprofit company. The ensemble seeks to recoup any remaining funds.</p><p>“Making art is about the artist. It's never about the board. We're still here. We are still an ensemble,” said Days.</p><p>The group has filed for a new 501(c)(3). And this time, they are prepared: Days said they are working with layers to add ensemble rights into the new articles of incorporation.</p><p>Looking back, Days adds, “It feels as if we had a board who was not really in it for the arts. They dissolved without even telling the press. We have been an institution in Chicago for all these years. They did it quietly.”</p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: The Academy Is... releases first new album in 18 years, gears up for Warped Tour
entries.summary: <p>As William Beckett took the stage at Madison Square Garden in 2024, it all came rushing back.</p><p>The lead vocalist of The Academy Is… was there to join old pals Fall Out Boy for a special cover of the Barrington pop-punk band’s song “Slow Down.” But despite playing the holy grail of concert venues, Beckett was thinking about the small suburban Chicago basements, VFW halls and Fireside Bowl where the two groups spent many nights during a time when the emo/pop-punk scene felt like the actual soundtrack of the city.</p><p>“It was just so cool to reconnect with them and to see how much hasn't changed from the VFW Hall days,” Beckett recently said during a Zoom call from his home in Barrington, where the band is getting ready to release its new album “Almost There.” Out March 27, it’s the band’s first album in 18 years, and a lot of the tracks memorialize the moments when the city felt like the band’s oyster.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-680000" name="image-680000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Academy Is... Almost There Artwork.png" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4500eb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1226x688+0+267/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F15%2Fb56b64534ff78867331c94378596%2Fthe-academy-is-almost-there-artwork.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bd0c4a5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1226x688+0+267/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F15%2Fb56b64534ff78867331c94378596%2Fthe-academy-is-almost-there-artwork.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4500eb5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1226x688+0+267/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F12%2F15%2Fb56b64534ff78867331c94378596%2Fthe-academy-is-almost-there-artwork.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </figure> </div> </div><p>He’s not far from Barrington High School, where The Academy Is… was founded in 2003 and soon wound up on a roller coaster of MTV and Warped Tour loops with aughts hits like “About a Girl” and “We’ve Got A Big Mess On Our Hands.” In fact, a lot of it is thanks to Fall Out Boy’s Pete Wentz, who championed The Academy Is… early on and helped to get the band signed to tastemaker label Fueled By Ramen. The two acts have been closely associated ever since. But for Beckett, that scene still feels as raw as day one. “It still exists in this way that is almost untouchable, like it exists beyond time.”</p><p>It’s a feeling that he and his The Academy Is… bandmates (bassist Adam Siska, guitarist Mike Carden and drummer Andy Mrotek) bottle up in their daydreamy new single “2005.” The lyrics throw it back to that summer, with Beckett singing about driving downtown while listening to Saves The Day’s 2001 opus "Stay What You Are.” At the same time, he wonders about bringing “TAI back from the dead,” which they effectively do with “Almost There.”</p><p>The seeds for the album were planted during a series of recent reunion shows, including Riot Fest 2022 and the elder millennial gathering When We Were Young in 2023 — the band’s first since calling it quits in 2015. And the band members found themselves looking back at the good ol’ days and wondering if they ever really appreciated it all.</p><p>“I think a lot of people have run away from that scene or from that feeling. And I always thought, ‘Man, it’d be so interesting to do a record that still has that spirit of what that was and celebrate it,’” said Carden who joined the Zoom chat from his current home base in Arizona. <b></b> To that effect, the band has started digitizing some of the VHS tapes from back in the day, recently finding footage from a gig at the Elk Grove Teen Center, while some members troll eBay for promotional relics. <b></b></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f90000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Academy Is... bassist Adam Siska (right) and lead vocalist William Beckett walk outside Navy Pier. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“It really was a magical time. Or maybe I just have this romanticized perspective of the pre-phone era, VHS tapes and the internet at the cusp of dial-up,” Carden joked. “But being part of that community and looking back, especially as we age, it’s like, ‘Wow, we were so lucky to have all that.’”</p><p><b></b> The wanderlust continues on another new song “L Train.” On its official social media pages, the band has been sharing behind-the-scenes footage of Beckett at stations around the city — although it’s not entirely accurate.</p><p>“This is what’s embarrassing for me. I’ve been in the suburbs forever, so I drive into the city instead of using the trains. Maybe it’s a form of masochism where I just need to have that I-90 bumper-to-bumper traffic because that’s what’s truly Chicago for me,” he joked. Yet, if the group had to pick a favorite line, “it would definitely be the Blue Line,” Carden said. “That’s the one we would take most often.” <b></b></p><p>While much has changed for the band with the advent of families, gray hairs and new fans who weren’t even alive the first time around, there’s still a full-circle feeling for The Academy Is… in 2026. And it’s not just because they’re playing the revitalized Warped Tour again this summer. “Almost There” is in many ways a continuation of the band’s 2005 debut “Almost Here,” with the road ahead a bit clearer this time<b>.</b></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bf0000"
