that with this record, we were able to convey a level of self-awareness and honesty and modesty,” said Beckett. “With the benefit of reflection about all these life experiences and personal growth with the peaks and valleys, I think it puts us in a situation where we may not know the destination, but we know that we’re on the right path.”</p>
entries.author: Selena Fragassi

entries.title: Thunder and rain could roll in for Cubs' Opening Day at Wrigley Field
entries.summary: <p>Anyone heading to Wrigley Field for the Cubs season opener on Thursday afternoon should be ready to spend some time watching the ground crew roll the tarp onto the field.</p><p>The National Weather Service says rain, hail and thunderstorms could hit the city sometime during the Cubs’ game against the Washington Nationals. Temperatures could start in the mid 50s then fall into the mid 40s. There is an 80% chance of rain.</p><p>The weather won’t be an issue for South Side fans heading to Milwaukee to see the White Sox start the season on the road against the Brewers, thanks to American Family Field’s retractable roof.</p><p>The Cubs-Nationals game is scheduled to start at 1:20 p.m. Matthew Boyd will start for the Cubs and Cade Cavalli is going for the Nationals.</p><p>Former Cubs great Anthony Rizzo will throw out the ceremonial first pitch as the club starts the year celebrating the 10th anniversary of its World Series championship and the franchise’s 150th anniversary.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times staff

entries.title: Inside Medicare Advantage ‘dark money’ group’s campaign to win bigger payments to insurers
entries.summary: <p>Judging by more than 16,400 comments recently posted on a federal government website, you’d think there was a groundswell of older Americans demanding that federal officials hike payments to their <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/08/27/medicare-advantage-unitedhealth-cms-centers-medicare-medicaid-services-cheri-rice-anne-horsby-richard-lieberman-erin-fuse-brown" >Medicare Advantage</a> health insurance plans.</p><p>Yet about 82% of the comments are identical to a letter that appeared on the website of a secretive advocacy group called Medicare Advantage Majority, a data analysis by <a class="Link" href="https://kffhealthnews.org/news/article/medicare-advantage-rates-public-comments-industry-ads-facebook-dark-money/" target="_blank" ><u>KFF Health News has found.</u></a></p><p>The “<a class="Link" href="https://www.opensecrets.org/dark-money/basics" target="_blank" ><u>dark money</u></a>” group — which doesn’t make public who its funders are or much else — says it’s “dedicated to protecting and strengthening Medicare Advantage” and is “powered by hundreds of thousands of local advocates nationwide.”</p><p>“Our campaign provides information and offers tools for concerned Americans to use to reach decision-makers,” spokesperson Darren Grubb said.</p><p>The group has spent more than $3.1 million on hundreds of Facebook ads since September 2024, according to <a class="Link" href="https://www.facebook.com/ads/library/?active_status=active&ad_type=political_and_issue_ads&country=US&is_targeted_country=false&media_type=all&sort_data%5bmode%5d=total_impressions&sort_data%5bdirection%5d=desc" target="_blank" ><u>Facebook’s Ad Library</u></a>, a database of the social media company’s online ads.</p><p>There’s no doubt health insurers are unhappy with a proposal from the federal Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services in January that would keep Medicare Advantage reimbursement rates essentially flat in 2027 — far less than they expected from the Trump administration.</p><p>Medicare Advantage plans differ from traditional Medicare, which is run by the federal government, because private insurance companies administer them. The insurance plans enroll about 35 million members, more than half of all people eligible for Medicare. The plans offer things like vision and drug coverage, but Medicare Advantage insurers restrict which hospitals and doctors patients can use and require prior approval for various procedures.</p><p>CMS is set to announce a final decision by early next month on the rate proposal. The agency solicited <a class="Link" href="https://www.regulations.gov/docket/CMS-2026-0034" target="_blank" ><u>public comments</u></a> on the proposal from Jan. 26 through Feb. 25 to give interested parties and the public a chance to air their views.</p><p>Medicare Advantage Majority, which says the rate proposal amounts to a “cut” in services and warns of dire consequences for seniors if it goes through, accounted for at least 13,522 of the 16,422 comments published as of March 12.</p><p>The proposed rate plan “puts my access to care at risk,” the group’s template letter to policymakers reads in part. “If the investment made by Washington in the Medicare Advantage program is nearly flat year-over-year, I could lose benefits I rely on every day, including affordable prescriptions, capped out of pocket costs, and access to trusted doctors and specialists.”</p><p>“Medicare Advantage is not optional for me,” the letter says. “The cost protections alone have saved me thousands of dollars and made my health care manageable. Without this program, I would face higher costs, fewer providers, and fewer benefits at a time when I can least afford it.”</p><p>Critics say these sorts of campaigns might create a misleading impression of grassroots support, especially when it’s not clear who’s financing them.</p><p>“It puts a different spin on a massive groundswell of comments to know all are being driven by one specific organization,” said Michael Beckel, director of money in politics reform for Issue One, a group that aims to limit the influence of money on government policy and legislation. “There’s no way for the public to know what wealthy donors or special interests are funding dark money groups like this. That means there’s no scrutiny of who’s really calling the shots.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2026/01/29/nx-s1-5691896/medicare-advantage-health-insurance-trump-oz-cms-upcoding" target="_blank" >Medicare Advantage insurers face new curbs on overcharges in Trump plan</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><h3>What’s at stake for insurers</h3><p>Some health care policy experts, who have argued that the government overpays Medicare Advantage plans by tens of billions of dollars every year, say industry groups or their surrogates routinely overstate possible negative impacts of rate decisions they don’t like.</p><p>“The plans always say that the sky is falling,” said Matthew Fiedler, a health care policy expert with the Brookings Institution. “The industry has a lot of money at stake here. They try to exert pressure on policymakers any way they can.”</p><p>But even critics concede that some of the millions of people enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans could face service cuts if insurance companies aren’t satisfied with government payments.</p><p>“It is legitimate for people to be worried,” said Julie Carter, counsel for federal policy for the Medicare Rights Center, a group that advocates for older adults and people with disabilities.</p><p>Her group argues that Medicare Advantage plans never have attained expected cost savings and instead have been overpaid for years at least partly due to “actions to maximize profits.” She said the health plans “are supposed to be saving money, not taking extra.”</p><p>People struggling to pay health care bills might have little use for the policy debate.</p><p>“If it wasn’t for being able to have this program, I really wouldn’t be able to afford any kind of medical services, to be honest,” said EsterAlicia Rose, 75, who works the front desk of a hotel in Pagosa Springs, Colorado, and said she signed the Medicare Advantage Majority form letter to reach policymakers.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2c0000" name="image-2c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" class="Figure-credit"><p>Sstock.adobe.com</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Perks and concerns</h3><p>Kathy Lovely-Marshall, 66, a retired nurse in Brookville, Ohio, did, too. She said she gets “a lot of perks” from her plan, including dental care, glasses and prescriptions.</p><p>“All those things are a big plus as far as I am concerned,” she said. “I’m very happy with the plan I have.”</p><p>But Corenia Branham, 90, a widow and cancer survivor who lives in Alum Creek, West Virginia, said she wants nothing to do with Medicare Advantage plans run by private health insurance companies. She said she didn’t turn in any of the four form letters under her name, which were posted online by CMS on Feb. 23 and signed, “Miss Corenia Branham Branham.” It’s not clear why her last name is signed twice.</p><p>Branham said she’s not on Medicare Advantage and doubts she could count on it for needed care.</p><p>“I wouldn’t recommend it to nobody,” she said. “I sure don’t want anything to do with it.”</p><p>Grubb disputed that account. He said Branham responded to an ad on Facebook. On Feb. 6, she “completed the form with her information and chose to send her comment to CMS as well as to her representatives in Congress and the White House,” the Medicare Advantage Majority spokesperson said.</p><p>Other Medicare Advantage advocacy groups have stepped up ad campaigns as the rate decision looms.</p><p>The Better Medicare Alliance, whose “allies” include a range of health insurers, health care providers, and consumers, is urging seniors to “Tell Washington to Stand Up for Medicare Advantage.”</p><p>“We’ve mobilized beneficiaries to write letters and make phone calls, and we’ve run digital ads on streaming platforms,” spokesperson Susan Reilly said.</p><p>Reilly said roughly 3 million seniors this year “were forced to find new coverage” because plans either shuttered operations or left some areas.</p><p>She said Medicare Advantage plans have “scaled back” benefits such as offering transportation to medical appointments, nutrition support and dental and vision coverage even as their customers, over the past two years, have faced an average $900 increase in out-of-pocket maximums.</p><p>“We do view this as especially serious,” Reilly said. “This isn’t a single bad year. It’s the cumulative effect of years of underfunding and policy disruption from the previous administration that has left the program increasingly vulnerable.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/12/27/fighting-health-insurance-denial-doctor-medicare-medicaid-tips" >Fighting a health insurance denial? that should have been paid, HHS finds</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>As of March 12, CMS said it had received 46,884 comments but had posted only 16,422 online.</p><p>CMS spokesperson Catherine Howden said impact of comments while the comment period is open/under review,” she said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://kffhealthnews.org/about-us" target="_blank" ><i><u>KFF Health News</u></i></a><i> is a entries.title: 49th Ward office closes temporarily after violent threats following killing of Loyola student in Rogers Park
entries.summary: <p>The 49th Ward's office has closed for the rest of the week due to "threats of violence" to the alderperson and her staff following backlash to comments she made after the homicide of 18-year-old Loyola student Sheridan Gorman. </p><p>In a letter to constituents Wednesday, Ald. Maria Hadden (49th) announced her office was offering services remotely Wednesday and Thursday before closing entirely Friday due to "threats of violence against myself, my family, and my team."</p><p>Hadden said the threats involved "racist, misogynistic and homophobic language" and verbal abuse to her staff was "making it difficult to serve the constituents of the 49th Ward."</p><p>Hadden appeared on "Chicago Live" the same day Gorman was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/heres-what-to-know-killing-sheridan-gorman" target="_blank" >shot and killed</a> for an interview that circulated in conservative news cycles and social media groups.</p><p>During the interview, Hadden was asked if members of the 49th Ward should be worried about their safety. </p><p>"They were just out, people go out to the beach all the time," Hadden responded as she described what she knew from police. "The kids were out doing normal things people do in the neighborhood, and it sounds like this might've been a wrong place, wrong time, running into a person who had a gun, they might've startled this person at the end of the pier unintentionally." </p><p>The fatal shooting garnered national attention, including from<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-trump-sheridan-gorman-loyola-murder-shooting-deportation-immigration" target="_blank" > President Donald Trump</a>, after a Venezuelan migrant was charged with Gorman's murder. The shooting created an increased political discourse regarding immigration enforcement in Chicago, where elected officials have resisted enhanced enforcement tactics. </p><p>"Gun violence and immigration policy have been two of the most challenging issues facing our country and our city. Sheridan's murder has become a national story touching on these painful and difficult issues," Hadden said in her newsletter. "I know that people are in pain, and tensions will be high as we work to find solutions to keep everyone in our community safe."</p><p>Hadden declined to elaborate on the backlash when reached by a Sun-Times reporter Thursday. </p><p>Following the interview, Hadden said her office received "an overwhelming number" of calls and emails, mostly from people out-of-state "who are angry about my comments and Chicago's sanctuary city status."</p><p>"Our social media pages have also been inundated with negative reviews and hateful comments. The content of the phone messages and emails that my staff and I have been receiving is explicit and disturbing," Hadden wrote. </p><p>An anti-immigrant, anti-sanctuary city protest took place outside the Rogers Park office Tuesday and another was planned for Friday, according to Hadden. </p><p>The 49th Ward office was expected to fully reopen Monday. </p><p>"I ask that here, in the 49th Ward, we bring compassion, patience, and understanding to debates and discussions we have with our neighbors about this in the coming weeks," Hadden wrote. <br> </p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Funeral for Chicago firefighter killed in Rogers Park blaze canceled after wife goes into labor
entries.summary: <p>The funeral for Michael Altman, a Chicago firefighter killed in a Rogers Park blaze earlier this month, was canceled after his wife went into labor Thursday. </p><p>The funeral, scheduled for Friday, will be rescheduled for a later date, according to the Chicago Fire Department. Visitation was still scheduled to take place Thursday from 3 p.m. to 8 p.m. at the St. Rita of Cascia Shrine Chapel, 7740 S. Western Ave.</p><p>Altman was among the firefighters who <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/16/extra-alarm-blaze-erupts-at-a-rogers-park-building-possibly-injuring-firefighter" target="_blank" >responded to a fire</a> behind a four-story building in the 1700 block of West North Shore Avenue that started around 11:20 a.m. March 16, Deputy District Fire Chief Kelly Burns told reporters at the scene. Burns said he fell through a floor that collapsed.</p><p>Sheaves Slate, 27, was charged with murder, residential arson and aggravated arson injuring a firefighter in connection to the fire and was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/23/chicago-crime-fire-michael-altman-cfd-sheaves-slate" target="_blank" >ordered detained at a court hearing Monday. </a></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Bulls' Josh Giddey hopes roster can avoid splintering down the stretch
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA – Josh Giddey knows the direction this can go.</p><p>A glimpse at the standings, a few thoughts creep into the head, and later that night a Bulls player finds himself going iso on a defender he has very little chance to beat off the dribble while a teammate is wide open for a corner three.</p><p>That’s how a team splinters, that’s how an already bad season turns downright miserable, and that’s what Giddey saw in the humiliating performance against the 76ers on Wednesday.</p><p>“It’s kind of a weird spot,” Giddey said. “There’s a lot of free agents in the room, so a lot of guys have contract situations coming up, but I think the most important thing is we keep playing the right way.”</p><p>Giddey was speaking from experience. He was dealing with a very public contract year last season, and his 2024-25 campaign couldn’t have started off worse. He was benched late in several games because of defensive breakdowns in November, felt like he was failing himself and his new team by December, and finally started to put it together after the All-Star break.</p><p>And while most NBA players dismiss the contract as a mental obstacle, Giddey was very transparent in admitting that it was weighing on him. He did eventually get his four-year, $100-million deal done in the summer, but even now he can recall the emotional roller coaster he rode.</p><p>“I’m not going to speak for other guys because I know how it is to be in a contract year and individually you are always worried about yourself,” Giddey said. “Everybody wants to play well, that’s no secret, contract-wise or not, especially coming into free agency. You want to perform, you want your visibility to be as high as possible, so I get it from that point of view. But it’s important to make everything about the team. It’s easy for guys to waver and think about themselves. I was guilty of it as well.</p><p>“I just think it’s important to let that stuff get handled in the offseason. Don’t drag it into the team. (Wednesday) in stints I thought we played selfish ball and started to get in one-on-one stuff, iso stuff, and it’s just not the way we play. It’s not sustainable and not how we have to win games.”</p><p>As it stands right now, Anfernee Simons, Collin Sexton, Nick Richards, Zach Collins and Guerschon Yabusele are all unrestricted free agents. Jaden Ivey is a restricted free agent, while the Bulls have a team-friendly option on Leonard Miller.</p><p>That could be a lot of moving parts yet again.</p><p>Giddey & Co. already went through this exercise, starting this season off with a locker room full of free agents with Coby White, Ayo Dosunmu, Nikola Vucevic and Kevin Huerter headlining that group.</p><p>To their credit, that first iteration was able to compartmentalize their own situation from the team’s well-being.</p><p>This current group? Maybe not so much.</p><p>Then again, the losing has come much more frequently since the roster was flipped, there’s questions about the coaching future of Billy Donovan, and within a few more games there will be nothing left to play for with the Bulls on the brink of play-in game elimination.</p><p>“I put myself in their shoes because I know what it’s like to have that in the back of your mind,” Giddey added. “Everybody wants to take care of their families, you got money to make, so I get that from a contractual standpoint, and while we’re out of the playoffs – not yet, but we might as well be – it’s important we stay together, keep doing this thing together, and let that stuff be handled in the summer.”</p>
entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: A West Suburban Medical emergency
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: In a shock to staff and patients, West Suburban Medical Center is closing its doors temporarily and furloughing "many" employees.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: The city's human relations commissioner resigns in protest, the mayor vetoes a plan to freeze the pay of tipped workers, baseball is back — and more news you need to know. </p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-y/" target="_blank" >The Bulls suffered a blowout loss to the 76ers</a>, 157-137.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" target="_blank" >Get this newsletter delivered to your inbox weekday mornings.</a></p><p>⏱️: <i>An 8-minute read</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY’S WEATHER 🌤️</h4><h4></h4><p>Mostly sunny early on with a chance of showers, wind gusts up to 30 mph and a high near 70. temporarily as owner blames medical record system</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-d/" target="_blank" >Kaitlin Washburn</a></h5><p><b>Medical emergency</b>: West Suburban Medical Center is temporarily shutting its doors and furloughing "many" employees as it struggles to pay staff, according to the Oak Park hospital’s owner, Manoj Prasad. He did not say when the safety net hospital, which has 234 beds, would reopen.</p><p><b>The scope</b>: The hospital’s emergency room, inpatient units and clinics are closing "effective immediately," Prasad said. He blamed the hospital’s year-old electronic medical record system "that has never functioned correctly" for the payroll issues. </p><p><b>Key context</b>: This comes seven months after Resilience Healthcare shuttered its other facility, Weiss Memorial Hospital in Uptown. Like Weiss, West Suburban serves a high percentage of patients who rely on Medicaid health insurance for people with low incomes or on Medicare for seniors, according to the most recent state data.</p><p><b>Big shock</b>: At the hospital Wednesday afternoon, some patients arriving for appointments were surprised to hear it was closing. Sanyaa Thomas, a hospital employee, said the news was a big shock to her and her co-workers. "It was still morning. And as we’re helping patients out, we just get an email that we were suspending patient care," she said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-h/" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-k/" target="_blank" >Human relations commissioner resigns after mayor’s office meddles with antisemitism report</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-u/" target="_blank" >Fran Spielman</a></h5><p><b>Commish out</b>: Nancy Andrade, the city's human relations commissioner, has resigned to protest what critics contend was an attempt by Mayor Brandon Johnson’s administration to whitewash a long-awaited report that was supposed to focus solely on antisemitism.</p><p><b>The report</b>: Human Relations Commission member Dan Goldwin said the report, triggered by a 58% rise in reported anti-Jewish hate crimes from 2023 to 2024, was completed and focused solely on the surge in incidents of antisemitism in Chicago — and what to do about it. But when it was forwarded to the mayor’s office, a consultant was hired to alter the report and turn it into a broader document on all hate incidents, Goldwin said.</p><p><a class="Link" target="_blank" >Mayor Johnson vetoes plan to freeze subminimum wage for tipped workers</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-n/" target="_blank" >Fran Spielman</a></h5><p><b>Third veto</b>: The mayor on Wednesday used his third veto to bury an ordinance that would have frozen the hourly pay of Chicago’s tipped workers at 76% of the minimum wage, and he’s likely to make it stick unless there’s a compromise to give restaurants a short break.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Last week, the City Council voted 30-18 to maintain the subminimum wage to throw an economic lifeline to struggling restaurants. That was four short of the 34 votes needed to override Mayor Johnson’s veto. alt="Dancers Ngozi Mokwunye (left) and Taylor Jackson rehearse choreographer Rena Butler's "Her Table" piece at Red Clay Dance Company in Woodlawn on the South Side, Wednesday, March 18, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/54254c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+317/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Fc9%2Faae74ac940d7b0694fd0295dbc1e%2Frenabutler-260327-013.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eef6e2f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+317/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Fc9%2Faae74ac940d7b0694fd0295dbc1e%2Frenabutler-260327-013.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/54254c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+317/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2d%2Fc9%2Faae74ac940d7b0694fd0295dbc1e%2Frenabutler-260327-013.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Ngozi Mokwunye, left, and Taylor Jackson rehearse choreographer Rena Butler’s “Her Table” at Red Clay Dance Company in Woodlawn.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-jy/" target="_blank" >Chicago-born choreographer honors Black womanhood with new dance</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-jj/" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a></h5><p>Choreographer Rena Butler’s connection to Chicago runs deep.</p><p>She has garnered meaningful experiences in the city, from the halls of her alma mater, the Chicago Academy of the Arts, to the stage of the Lyric Opera House, where she has presented her work. But some of her most formative moments took place at the kitchen table in her grandmother’s South Side home.</p><p>Butler will honor that sacred space in her new work, "Her Table," which will premiere March 28 during La Femme Dance Festival at Harris Theater for Music and Dance. Presented by Red Clay Dance Company, the biennial event showcases diverse works by Black women, who are often underrepresented on large stages.</p><p>The work is inspired by "The Kitchen Table Series," a seminal 1990 photo project by artist Carrie Mae Weems, who is Black. The set of black-and-white images depicts Weems in different scenes around her kitchen table — embracing a lover, getting her hair brushed by her mother, applying makeup with her young daughter, conversing with friends, even sitting in solitude.</p><p>"I was seeing myself in a museum for the first time," Butler, 36, said of first encountering Weems' work. "I was looking back at myself and my grandmother and my mother and my auntie and my sisters. And it just encapsulated so much lineage and a breadth of differing women within the Black community."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wthjiud-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>What's your defining memory of Opening Day in Chicago? ⚾</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Email us your answer.</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <u>What animal do you get most excited to see at a zoo, and why?</u></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"My late wife loved seeing penguins any time they appeared on TV. I tried to convince her to do a Penguin Encounter, but she never did. In her memory, I went to Brookfield Zoo last spring and signed up for an Encounter, so I had the chance to meet several of these adorable creatures. So very memorable." — Paul Lockwood </p><p>"The big cats. They behave so much like my house cat, I want to pet them." — Marianne Goss</p><p>"Always the majestic polar bear. Largest land carnivore, rare, endangered and beautiful." — Sharyn Rose Gocal</p><p>"Koalas, because when I was growing up there was nowhere in the Midwest that you could see them." — Laurie Alfaro</p><hr><p></p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-800000" name="image-800000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0554c39/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F28%2Fc34cb727415a874db1b78b070a8c%2Fsnowplow-260326-09.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4701d8a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F28%2Fc34cb727415a874db1b78b070a8c%2Fsnowplow-260326-09.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0fccec8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+316/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4e%2F28%2Fc34cb727415a874db1b78b070a8c%2Fsnowplow-260326-09.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Mayor Brandon Johnson speaks at a press conference at the unveiling of a newly named snowplow, Abolish Ice, at 2502 W. entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Boy, 17, critically wounded in Back of the Yards shooting
entries.summary: <p>Police are looking for three attackers, one of whom opened fire on a 17-year-old boy, leaving him in critical condition early Thursday in the Back of the Yards on the South Side. </p><p>The shooting happened around 12:30 a.m. in the 4300 block of South Ashland Avenue when three individuals approached the teen and one opened fire, Chicago police said. </p><p>The boy was shot multiple times in the body and was taken to Stroger Hospital where he was in critical condition, police said. </p><p>Area 1 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Legisladores estatales avanzan ley para mantener los centros de detención de inmigrantes fuera de comunidades
entries.summary: <p>Los legisladores estatales están tomando medidas para, en el futuro, mantener los centros de detención de inmigrantes fuera de las comunidades.</p><p>Bajo un proyecto de ley que salió de un comité de la Cámara de Illinois el miércoles, se prohibiría la construcción de nuevos centros de detención de inmigrantes dentro de un radio de 1,500 pies de escuelas, iglesias, centros de cuidado infantil, cementerios, parques públicos, reservas forestales, residencias privadas y vivienda pública</p><p>En apoyo a la legislación testificaron el presidente de la Cámara, Emanuel “Chris” Welch, y la alcaldesa de Broadview, Katrina Thompson, ambos representantes del área de Broadview, donde funciona un centro de procesamiento del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) que fue muy utilizado por ICE durante el Operativo Midway Blitz el otoño pasado.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ba0000" name="module-ba0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El proyecto de ley obtuvo un amplio apoyo de fue apoyado unánimemente por los demócratas, pero fue unánimemente rechazado por los republicanos.</p><p>“Este proyecto de ley dice algo muy simple y muy razonable: Las instalaciones de detención no pertenecen en medio de nuestros vecindarios”, dijo Welch. “No deberían estar al lado de escuelas, no deberían estar al lado de centros de cuidado infantil, no deberían estar al lado de parques, vivienda pública, lugares de culto o casas privadas como lo hace el centro de detención de Broadview”.</p><p>Welch destacó que a menos de 1,500 pies de la instalación de Broadview, hay dos centros de cuidado infantil, un parque, un lugar de culto y varias residencias privadas. El proyecto de ley no cerraría el actual centro de Broadview.</p><p>Broadview fue el epicentro de muchas protestas en contra de ICE durante la campaña de deportación. Seis manifestantes, apodados <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/16/chicago-broadview-six-kat-abughazaleh-midway-blitz-trump-ice" target="_blank" >“Los Seis de Broadview”</a>, fueron acusados de conspiración, un delito federal, tras su participación en protestas en la instalación. Dos de los seis han tenido los cargos desestimados.</p><p>“Para una comunidad pequeña como la nuestra, esto no es solo un número, es una carga significativa para nuestros contribuyentes, y sin embargo, no ha habido reembolso”, dijo Thompson.</p><p>El representante Christopher Davidsmeyer, republicano de Jacksonville, votó en contra de la legislación, calificándola de “proyecto de ley política”. Espera que sea impugnada en las cortes si se aprueba.</p><p>“La realidad es que nuestras leyes han estado empujando las acciones de ICE en las direcciones que han estado tomando”, dijo Davidsmeyer. “Así que si simplemente trabajamos con ellos, las cosas podrían suceder pacíficamente y no tendríamos este problema”.</p><p>El proyecto de ley espera votos en la Cámara de Representantes y en el Senado. Varios miembros del comité pidieron ser añadidos como co-patrocinadores del proyecto de ley.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

entries.title: Every known federal prosecution in Chicago tied to Trump’s immigration blitz
entries.summary: <p>Federal prosecutors in Chicago have accused 32 known defendants of nonimmigration crimes tied to Operation Midway Blitz. Many were accused of assaulting or resisting federal agents or officers.</p><p>Twenty of those defendants have already been cleared. Grand jurors refused to indict at least three of the 20, and another member of that group was found not guilty at trial.</p><p>Meanwhile, prosecutors have entered into deferred-prosecution agreements with three additional defendants. In each of those cases, if that person abides by certain terms over a set period of time, the feds have agreed to drop the charges in that case as well.</p><p>The Chicago Sun-Times is tracking all of the known criminal cases tied to Operation Midway Blitz:</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6e0000" entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: 2 men charged with murder of businessman involved in United Center development project
entries.summary: <p>Two men were charged with the murder of a businessman who was involved in a United Center development project near the arena on the Near West Side. </p><p>Nassie Mason and Erving Harris, both of Chicago, were charged with first degree murder and possessing a weapon while being a felon in the death of 67-year-old Jerry Lewis, Chicago police said. Harris was also wanted on a warrant.</p><p>Mason, 28, and Harris, 31, allegedly approached Lewis as he walked outside his office just blocks from the arena in an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/24/man-67-gunned-down-near-united-center-on-near-west-side" target="_blank" >apparent ambush attack</a>, according to officials and his family.</p><p>Lewis, 67, of Matteson, was shot in the head around 12:50 p.m. Tuesday outside of the nonprofit’s office at 2127 W. Madison St., according to Chicago police. He was rushed to Stroger Hospital and pronounced dead.</p><p>After the shooting, police officers detained Mason and Harris as they allegedly walked away from the building’s front entrance, according to a police report. Both men have felony records and have spent time in prison.</p><p>The suspects, both of whom are from Chicago's West Side, were scheduled to appear in court Friday for a detention hearing. </p><p>Lewis was closely involved in the 1901 Project, a $7 billion development led by the United Center’s owners — the Wirtz and Reinsdorf families. The project aims to turn 55 acres surrounding the stadium into a full-fledged entertainment district.</p><p>“My father was a great man, and his game plan was that he wanted change,” Zach Lewis told the Sun-Times Wednesday. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: CPS Board selects Macquline King as permanent CEO, will vote on her contract Monday
entries.summary: <p>The Chicago Board of Education will vote on Monday to appoint Macquline King as the permanent leader of Chicago Public Schools, catapulting a Black woman with homegrown talent to the district’s top post 10 months after she was appointed to the interim role.</p><p>According to <a class="Link" href="https://www.cpsboe.org/content/documents/march_30_2026_special_board_meeting_public_agenda_to_post.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>documents posted on the CPS Board’s website</u></a> Thursday, the school board has offered King the job and she has accepted it.</p><p>The proposed contract would be effective July 1 through June 30, 2029 — meaning she would lead the district well past the transition to a fully elected school board. If approved, King’s salary will start at $380,000, more than former CEO Pedro Martinez was offered. He was granted a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2021/10/25/22744850/cps-ceo-pedro-martinez-salary-contract-public-school#:~:text=CPS%20CEO%20Pedro%20Martinez%20to,year%20contract%20%2D%20Chicago%20Sun%2DTimes" target="_blank" ><u>$340,000 annual salary on a five-year contract.</u></a></p><p>This brings to the end a <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/03/13/cps-board-names-3-finalists-to-lead-district-interim-ceo-king-former-nyc-east-baton-rouge-schools-chiefs" target="_blank" ><u>protracted search</u></a> for a new leader of the school district. It started last year after <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2024/12/20/cps-ceo-pedro-martinez-threatens-to-sue-school-board-before-they-consider-firing-him-at-friday-meeting" target="_blank" ><u>Martinez was fired</u></a>.</p><p>Though the contract appoints her as CEO, King has a superintendent’s license, which the partly elected, partly appointed board <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/03/20/cps-school-board-votes-to-require-next-leader-be-an-educator-not-a-ceo" target="_blank" ><u>made a requirement for the position</u></a>. Those who have worked with King say she is an expert on teaching and learning, as well as building relationships and cultivating leadership.</p><p>Unlike some past CPS leaders, she does not have a background in accounting and will likely have to find a strong chief financial officer to help her navigate the district’s budget and debt. CPS is projected to face a $500 million deficit next school year, as well as future deficits.</p><p>King started as a teacher in CPS and became a principal of Dumas Elementary School on the South Side in 2007. She served there until CPS closed the school in 2013. She then became principal of a North Side elementary, which took in students from a nearby closed school.</p><p>She applied for the CEO position in 2021, but was passed over for Martinez. Mayor Lori Lightfoot hired King in 2022 to work in her office as the deputy education mayor.</p><p>Brandon Johnson kept King on when he became mayor. One of Johnson’s former top officials, who did not want to be named because they were not authorized to speak on the matter, called King a “wonderful person who was really well-liked.”</p><p>Though the school board is partially elected, Johnson still appoints the majority of its members.</p><p>As interim CEO, King immediately had to confront a budget deficit that <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/06/26/interim-chicago-public-schools-ceo-pegs-deficit-at-730-million" target="_blank" ><u>she pegged at more than $700 million</u></a>. She gained the respect of some of Johnson’s opponents by <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2025/08/14/majority-of-cps-board-demands-a-budget-with-pension-payment-and-loan-rebuffing-proposal-from-cps-leaders" target="_blank" ><u>refusing to present a budget</u></a> that included borrowing, which was something that the mayor’s office argued the budget should allow for.</p><p>King <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/11/05/cps-ceo-search-macquline-king" target="_blank" ><u>wasn’t among the finalists in the fall</u></a>, which became a point of contention among some groups who supported her, such as the Westside Branch of the NAACP. But her standing among board members has risen over the last few months as she’s handled <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-school-board/2025/08/28/cps-board-denies-johnsons-preferred-budget-rejects-pension-payment-and-loan" target="_blank" ><u>tense budget discussions,</u></a> the closings of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/19/cps-revokes-aspiras-ability-to-operate-charter-schools-in-chicago-in-rare-move-board-of-education" target="_blank" ><u>privately managed public schools</u></a> and widespread <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/12/no-recess-lockdowns-less-learning-how-immigration-agents-disrupted-cps-schools-this-fall" target="_blank" ><u>immigration enforcement that affected schools.</u></a> Board member Jitu Brown has said serving in the interim post has been like an “on the job interview” for King.</p><p>The board named three finalists earlier this month, but one of the candidates, Meisha Porter, the former leader of New York City’s school system, dropped out without offering a reason days later.</p><p>The other finalist was Sito Narcisse, the former head of Louisiana's second-largest school district, whose past job experience was somewhat polarizing. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/03/24/finalist-for-cps-ceo-sito-narcisse-courted-controversy-in-past-jobs-but-defenders-praise-his-vision" target="_blank" ><u>Some praised him as a “transformational leader”</u></a> but others criticized his leadership style.</p><p>There were some concerns among community members that the <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2026/03/18/chicago-public-schools-lacks-latino-ceo-finalists/" target="_blank" ><u>finalists didn’t include a Latino candidate</u></a>. Chalkbeat Chicago reported that a fourth finalist dropped out, but board members wouldn’t say if that person was Latino.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Alcalde de Chicago presenta camión quitanieve nombrado 'Abolish ICE'
entries.summary: <p>El alcalde Brandon Johnson y funcionarios de la ciudad presentaron el miércoles el quitanieve “Abolish ICE”, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/24/abolish-ice-entre-los-ganadores-de-concurso-para-nombrar-camiones-quitanieves-de-chicago" target="_blank" >ganador del concurso de nombramiento de quitanieves de este año</a>, que atrajo un número récord de propuestas.</p><p>El alcalde dijo que el nombre del quitanieve ganador es un mensaje al gobierno federal de que el Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) no es bienvenido en Chicago.</p><p>Sin embargo, la presentación en el vecindario West Town ocurre en un momento en que Chicago se ha convertido nuevamente en un punto focal del debate sobre las políticas de inmigración, tras el tiroteo fatal de la semana pasada de una estudiante de 18 años.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-110000" name="module-110000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La estudiante de primer año de la Universidad Loyola de Chicago, Sheridan Gorman, fue asesinada cerca del campus de Rogers Park. El presunto tirador, José G. Medina, es un inmigrante venezolano que fue detenido por la Patrulla Fronteriza en mayo de 2023, liberado y luego arrestado nuevamente meses después por cargos de robo en tienda.</p><p>Una persona interrumpió en el evento del miércoles reprendió al alcalde por presentar el quitanieve menos de una semana después de la muerte de Gorman.</p><p>"¡Estás burlándote de Sheridan Gorman!" gritó. "¡Debería darte vergüenza!”.</p><p>“Mis condolencias a la familia de Sheridan. Esta es una tragedia terrible”, dijo Johnson. “Y lo que he dicho desde el principio, continuaré utilizando todas las herramientas disponibles para proteger a los residentes de la ciudad de Chicago. Y esta tragedia no nos disuadirá de nuestro trabajo. De hecho, nos desafiará a todos a redoblar esfuerzos para garantizar que protejamos a cada individuo en todos los vecindarios”.</p><p>El cuarto concurso anual “Nombra un quitanieve” regresó en diciembre y recibió 13,300 propuestas. El lema “Abolish ICE” (Abolir ICE), dirigido al Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) fue propuesto alrededor de 9,300 veces, o aproximadamente el 70% de las entradas.</p><p>La avalancha de propuestas “Abolish ICE” siguió a varios meses tensos en Chicago, durante los cuales agentes federales de inmigración recorrieron la ciudad haciendo arrestos y utilizando gas lacrimógeno y otros irritantes químicos contra los manifestantes. Agentes federales le dispararon fatalmente a un hombre, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/10/no-olvidemos-el-asesinato-de-silverio-villegas-gonzalez" target="_blank" >Silverio Villegas Gonzalez</a>, e hirieron a una mujer, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >Marimar Martínez</a>, en incidentes separados.</p><p>Las protestas ocurrieron regularmente por toda la ciudad y en la instalación de procesamiento de ICE en el suburbio de Broadview.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/24/despliegan-a-agentes-de-ice-en-ohare-mientras-continua-el-cierre-parcial-del-gobierno-que-afecta-a-viajeros" target="_blank" >Los agentes de ICE también fueron desplegados</a> en aeropuertos de todo el país, incluyendo el Aeropuerto O'Hare, el lunes, en medio de un cierre parcial del gobierno que ha dejado a los oficiales de la Administración de Seguridad en el Transporte (TSA) trabajando sin salario durante semanas.</p><p>“Este nombre proviene del legado de nuestra ciudad de defender la justicia, la dignidad y los derechos de todas las personas, sin importar de dónde vengan”, aseguró Johnson. “Quiero aprovechar este momento para reiterar que Chicago no quiere a ICE en nuestras calles, en nuestros aeropuertos, ni en nuestra ciudad. Chicago cree en abolir a ICE”.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Zubaer Khan </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: On Opening Day, some Cubs fans lean into sports betting boom while others remain wary
entries.summary: <p>Logan Reilly’s group chat with his friends was lighting up Thursday, the same as every year, for Major League Baseball’s Opening Day.</p><p>But this time, the chatter was about which bets to place.</p><p>“Three years ago, we're just talking about who we think’s going to win,” Reilly said outside Wrigley Field ahead of the Chicago Cubs game against the Washington Nationals. “But now we're talking about do we think Nico Hoerner’s going to get on base? For the Mets game, do we think Juan Soto’s going to hit a home run?”</p><p>Reilly is among millions of fans who have jumped into the betting arena as the pastime has become inseparable from sports entertainment. Betting ads litter stadiums and television broadcasts. Sportsbooks are opening at ballparks including Wrigley Field. And gambling is as easy as ever on mobile apps.</p><p>Illinois legalized sports gambling in early 2020 and has seen participation soar in the years since. More than 26.3 million sports bets have already been placed in Illinois in 2026, the vast majority of them online, according to the Illinois Gaming Board.</p><p>“It's one of the most wild things I've ever seen,” Reilly said. “The demand for it has just absolutely skyrocketed.</p><p>“I don't bet too often. I bet when I'm confident, I'd say. But we all know how that goes sometimes,” he added. “If you feel that you're not doing it responsibly, then you should probably take a step back.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div Several recent scandals involving athletes altering their performance to win bets have rocked some fans’ confidence in the integrity of sports — though sports rigging scandals date back decades.</p><p>Jake Evans, 17, said sports betting has grown popular among his peers who are becoming more engaged with sports than they otherwise would have been.</p><p>“They feel more interested about watching sports, and they care more, especially because they're putting money on it,” he said. “And, obviously, they're bragging about winning money off of sports betting, which kind of piques people's interests.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-380000" name="image-380000"></a> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Lisa and Matt Neenam (far right) with her parents outside Wrigley. Matt Neenam thinks sports betting is too accessible. “It’s a casino in a pocket, which isn’t good for anybody,” he said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Matt Neenan, who was attending the Cubs game with his wife Lisa and her parents, said he has placed a couple bets before, but he worries about the growing prevalence of betting among young men in particular.</p><p>“It's a casino in a pocket, which isn't good for anybody,” he said. “I think it's getting people addicted to it because it's too easy and too accessible. So it's gamified and too easy for young people to get sucked into it.”</p><p>Fans attending games at Wrigley Field can bet on their phones like anyone else — or they can head down to the DraftKings Sportsbook that opened at the ballpark during the 2023 baseball season.</p><p>Reilly was sitting with his sister in Gallagher Way outside Wrigley Field a couple hours before the game while they waited for Reilly’s brother and mom, who had walked over to the sportsbook to place a few small bets for him.</p><p>What were his picks for the day?</p><p>“I went for a [Cubs third basemen Alex] Bregman hit, a Nico Hoerner hit and the Cubs to win,” he said. “Go Cubs.”<br></p>
entries.author: Nader Issa

entries.title: How Taylor McGregor turned public-speaking skills into broadcasting career
entries.summary: <p>Keli McGregor couldn’t have foreseen his daughter Taylor’s career in broadcasting before he died at 48 on April 20, 2010. The idea was still new to Taylor, who was a junior at Golden High School at the time. But her father, who had been the Rockies’ president since 2001, posthumously gave her a nudge.</p><p>Taylor spoke at Keli’s memorial service in front of an estimated 3,000 people at Coors Field. What she said and how she said it left such an impression on those gathered that people still mention it to her.</p><p>“That was really when everybody started coming up to me [saying], ‘You have a gift. You need to lean into whatever this is,’ ” said McGregor, who spoke after her two sisters and brother. “That was one of those, OK, I need to pursue something in this.”</p><p>That she did, and on Thursday, she began her seventh season as Marquee Sports Network’s lead field reporter on Cubs broadcasts. Last week, she worked her first NBA game for ESPN, expanding a national profile that added the College Football Playoff last season.</p><p>McGregor’s bosses at ESPN are noticing what Cubs fans — and those who saw her speak that day in Denver — already knew: She’s very comfortable in front of a crowd of thousands, let alone a camera before millions.</p><p>“So many of our bosses sit in the [production] truck, and I think they’ve recognized that I have a good idea of what’s interesting,” McGregor, 33, said. “And I worked hard at that. It takes time to understand the sport, to really dive in to be able to differentiate yourself.”</p><p>Initially, McGregor thought about differentiating herself, well, differently.</p><p>The 9/11 attacks happened when McGregor was in third grade, and her teacher, who was from New York, often would talk with the class about the events of the day. McGregor remembers thinking that she “wanted to help catch the bad guys.” One way to do that was to join the CIA.</p><p>When McGregor was in eighth grade, her father was running the Rockies. The team’s head of security was a former FBI agent who had a friend who was an active CIA agent. A meeting was arranged — she still doesn’t think she knows the guy’s real name — and she peppered him with questions, fascinated by the possibility.</p><p>“But the one downside I remember him telling me is, as a female, it’s really hard to do this because you probably won’t be able to have a family and kids because you’re going to be all over,” McGregor said. “And I remember thinking, Well, I really want a family and kids, so maybe this isn’t for me.”</p><p>Meanwhile, women were becoming more prevalent in sports television. McGregor watched a lot of Rockies games, so she saw Alanna Rizzo, the team’s field reporter, and Charissa Thompson, who hosted the team’s weekly all-access show. And she remembers thinking, I can do that.</p><p>McGregor had been performing in front of crowds in some fashion since she was a child. She was the lead in elementary school plays. She was in the children’s choir. She was an athlete, excelling in soccer. Why not in front of a camera?</p><p>After graduating from the University of Arkansas with a degree in broadcast journalism, McGregor began her career at KCWY-TV in Casper, Wyoming, making $18,000 a year. It was a grind. Once at a drive-thru Starbucks, her debit card was declined, and she had to call her mother and ask for cash.</p><p>But as the weekday sports anchor, she got a lot of reps, which are invaluable. She also learned how to be a reporter, covering high schools that didn’t come with sports-information departments.</p><p>Most importantly, McGregor learned how badly she wanted to do the job. After a stop at KTHV-TV in Little Rock, Arkansas, she became the Rockies’ field reporter on AT&T SportsNet Rocky Mountain, where she caught the attention of ESPN NFL analyst and former quarterback Brian Griese, a huge Rockies fan.</p><p>“I had never met Brian Griese,” McGregor said, “but one day at the ballpark, I run into him, and I introduced myself. I’m like, ‘Hey, I’m Taylor McGregor, and I wanna be a sideline reporter for ESPN.’ He said, ‘I watch your stuff, you can do it. I’ve worked with a lot of reporters; you’re as good if not better than them.’ ”</p><p>Unprompted, Griese called Steve Ackels, then ESPN’s coordinating producer for college football, who had told McGregor when they met at SEC media days that the network had no openings. But the next day, she got a call from Ackels, who offered her a few games in that 2019 season. After starting on an SEC Network overflow channel, she finished the season on ESPN.</p><p>Meanwhile, Marquee was preparing to launch in 2020, and McGregor got word that the network was looking for an experienced field reporter. At first, she wasn’t interested. She loved her job with the Rockies. She was home. Then she talked with some colleagues, who pointed to the Cubs’ draw and stature.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/760456a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F5b%2F107f15e04cfd993a49dbccf04df3%2Fdsc3616-1.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ffa05b8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6000x3367+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Faf%2F5b%2F107f15e04cfd993a49dbccf04df3%2Fdsc3616-1.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Jon “Boog” Sciambi, Taylor McGregor and Jim Deshaies form Marquee Sports Network’s top Cubs broadcast team.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Scott Changnon/Marquee</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“They were like, ‘You’re insane. You have to go after this,’ ” McGregor said. “I said, ‘OK, you’re right.’ I ended up flying out, and I remember landing in Chicago and being like, not only am I going to put myself up for this job, but I think I’m going to get this job.”</p><p>Deep down, McGregor felt she needed to branch out and be in a place where she wasn’t known mostly for being Keli McGregor’s daughter. Of course, she loved being known for that and was proud of her father’s legacy. But this was a way to start her own.</p><p>“Taylor was very credible and very capable right from the start,” said former Marquee general manager Mike McCarthy, who hired McGregor. “While she has a God-given talent for putting people at ease, she also has a strong confidence about her, without any arrogance or aloofness. We saw it on her tapes, and we saw it in our meetings with her. She was, and is, the real deal.”</p><p>McGregor, who now lives in Lincoln Park, is performing very well on Marquee’s top team with play-by-play voice Jon Sciambi and analyst Jim Deshaies. Her preparation is evident, her delivery is comfortable and her banter with her broadcast mates is delightful.</p><p>Keli would be proud.<br></p>
entries.author: Jeff Agrest

entries.title: Se quitan más murales y monumentos de César Chávez mientras el Senado de Illinois honra a Dolores Huerta
entries.summary: <p>Los murales y monumentos de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/19/acusaciones-de-abuso-de-cesar-chavez-entristecen-y-sorprenden-a-la-comunidad-latina-de-chicago" target="_blank" >César Chávez</a> continúan siendo retirados en Chicago tras las alegaciones que surgieron la semana pasada de que el activista por los derechos civiles latinos había preparado y agredido sexualmente a niñas y mujeres.</p><p>Las alegaciones fueron reveladas en una investigación del New York Times publicada el 18 de marzo.</p><p>Algunas obras de arte se están volviendo a pintar con murales de Dolores Huerta, la aliada de Chávez en el movimiento por los derechos laborales. Huerta, de 95 años, le dijo al New York Times que Chávez la había agredido sexualmente.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-5b0000" name="module-5b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>El Senado de Illinois adoptó una resolución el miércoles en honor a Huerta y declaró el 10 de abril como "Día de Dolores Huerta" en Illinois.</p><p>"En los últimos meses, Dolores Huerta ha mostrado una profunda resiliencia y valentía al compartir su propia experiencia de daño, haciéndolo para elevar las historias de innumerables mujeres cuyas voces fueron pasadas por alto o silenciadas", dice la resolución.</p><p>Uno de los patrocinadores de la resolución, la senadora estatal Celina Villanueva, demócrata por Chicago, dijo que también está trabajando en un plan para rescindir el Día de César Chávez, que se celebra la próxima semana, el 31 de marzo.</p><p>En Café Tola, en el vecindario de Lake View, un mural de Chávez en el exterior del restaurante fue reemplazado esta semana por una pintura de Huerta, quien acuñó la frase "Sí, se puede".</p><p>"Estamos profundamente entristecidos por esta noticia, pero nos mantenemos en un apoyo y admiración inquebrantables por Dolores Huerta y cada mujer que ha encontrado el coraje de hablar", dijo Café Tola en una publicación en redes sociales. "Este momento es poderoso, prueba de que la verdad no puede ser silenciada y que nunca es demasiado tarde para recuperar tu voz. Honramos esa fortaleza".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-63fc7d" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Una placa dedicada al 30 aniversario de la muerte de César Chávez, en honor a él y a la “Hermana Dolores Huerta”, antes de ser cubierta con cinta en el Memorial Haymarket en el West Loop.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>AP</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>En el West Loop, una placa en honor al movimiento por los derechos laborales de Chávez ha sido cubierta con cinta negra en el Memorial de Haymarket. Según la Federación de Trabajo de Chicago, la organización planea retirarla por completo.</p><p>"Discutiremos con todos nuestros socios cómo honrar mejor tanto a las sobrevivientes de los horribles abusos de César Chávez como a los trabajadores que fueron traicionados por él", dijo un portavoz de la Federación de Trabajo de Chicago en un comunicado.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Una cinta negra cubre una placa (izquierda) que conmemora el 30 aniversario de la muerte de César Chávez en la base del Memorial de Haymarket en el West Loop, miércoles 25 de marzo de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>La semana pasada, Bob Reiter, presidente de la Federación de Trabajo de Chicago, dijo que las alegaciones de que Chávez agredió sexualmente a niños, mujeres, trabajadores y organizadores laborales "son una traición a nuestro movimiento".</p><p>Chávez, quien murió en 1993, ha sido venerado durante mucho tiempo en la comunidad latina de Chicago. Además de numerosos murales de él en toda la ciudad, una escuela pública primaria en Back of the Yards y una oficina de correo en Pilsen llevan su nombre.</p><p>La escuela, César E. Chávez Multicultural Academic Center, ha iniciado el proceso que podría llevar a un nuevo nombre. Está recibiendo comentarios de la comunidad sobre un posible nuevo nombre, según funcionarios de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS). Cualquier recomendación necesitaría aprobación del Concilio Escolar Local (LSC) y de la junta escolar.</p><p>Otra escuela en Pilsen, la Peter Cooper Dual Language Academy, tiene un mosaico de Chávez y Huerta uno al lado del otro. No está claro si la escuela planea retirar el mosaico de Chávez.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/CxOFD7pr0Pa/" target="_blank" >https://www.instagram.com/p/CxOFD7pr0Pa/</a></p><p>El Sun-Times fue el primero en reportar que la cara de Chávez había sido <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/23/cubren-mural-de-cesar-chavez-en-parque-de-pilsen-tambien-piden-renombrar-edificios-publicos-en-chicago" target="_blank" >pintada la semana pasada</a> en el mural "Libertad" que se encuentra en una larga pared en Barrett Park en Pilsen.</p><p>Una mancha de pintura verde cubría inicialmente la parte donde Chávez estaba representado, pero desde entonces, los funcionarios del Distrito de Parques han vuelto a pintar esa sección con una continuación del diseño de fondo del mural, dijo un portavoz del Distrito de Parques.</p><p>Otras figuras históricas en el mural, incluyendo a la artista mexicana Frida Kahlo, Malcolm X, Rosa Parks y el reverendo Martin Luther King Jr., permanecen visibles en la pared.</p><div class="Enhancement" exterior de Barrett Park en el vecindario de Pilsen, el viernes 20 de marzo de 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Time</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“El Distrito de Parques de Chicago toma en serio las recientes alegaciones de conducta inapropiada de César Chávez... y está llevando a cabo una revisión en todo el distrito de cualquier otra característica del parque que pueda honrarlo", dijo anteriormente un portavoz del Distrito de Parques. "Donde sea apropiado, tomaremos acciones adicionales que sean coherentes con nuestros valores y estándares”.</p><p>El Distrito de Parques no ha identificado otros murales o memoriales de Chávez en ninguna de sus propiedades, dijo el portavoz el miércoles.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Isabela Nieto </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Kade Heather

entries.title: Mars Snacking, maker of M&M's, plans to add 600 jobs in Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Mars Snacking plans to invest $100 million to expand its global headquarters in Chicago and create more than 600 jobs.</p><p>Mars Snacking, a division of Virginia-based Mars, announced the investment Wednesday with Gov. JB Pritzker. The company will receive about $43 million in tax incentives, as part of the state's Economic Development for a Growing Economy program. </p><p>The deal comes three months after Mars, maker of M&M's and Snickers, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/money/2024/08/14/mars-buy-chicago-kellanova-maker-cheezits-pringles-nearly-30-billion" >finalized its acquisition</a> of Chicago-based snack company Kellanova, whose portfolio includes popular brands like Pringles, Pop-Tarts and Cheez-Its.</p><p>In addition to its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2024/1/18/24043275/maker-snickers-mandms-mars-unveils-chocolate-candy-innovation-hub-goose-island" >global headquarters</a> in Goose Island, Mars Snacking said it would add its North American regional office at <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2023/3/24/23655374/chan-zuckerberg-life-sciences-hub-open-fulton-market" >Fulton Labs</a>, 400 N. Aberdeen St. Its accelerator division will open a new global office at Kellanova's former headquarters in River North. </p><p>“Chicago has long been a hub for our business, and now it is our official home for our North America region and our Accelerator Division — firmly establishing our legacy and our future together,”<b> </b>Andrew Clarke, global president of Mars Snacking, said in a news release. “The Chicagoland area offers the best of all worlds for Mars Snacking — a vibrant urban environment, strong communities and access to world-class talent. We are proud to deepen our roots here, advance the Mars Snacking business, and continue our long-standing commitment to community engagement and responsible business.”</p><p>Its $100 million investment will be for project costs such as site renovations, purchasing buildings and research and development costs, according to the EDGE agreement.</p><p>Seventeen employees are slated to join the Fulton Market office, and 585 employees will join its Goose Island location, according to the agreement. The company has until Dec. 27, 2027, to hire the 602 employees.</p><p>Mars Snacking is required to retain a minimum of 562 employees whose jobs are “directly related to the project,” the agreement said.</p><p>The tax breaks are also contingent on Mars Snacking retaining at least 2,347 existing full-time employees in Illinois before it can claim tax credits for the new jobs. The company currently employs 4,000 people in the Chicago area, according to a news release.</p><p>The EDGE agreement dictates that if Mars Snacking shuts down operations within the first five years of receiving tax credits, the company may be required to refund the state.</p><p>The company is eligible for tax credits for 15 consecutive years, according to the agreement. The annual credits are typically determined by the incremental income tax withheld from new employees’ pay stubs. And Mars Snacking can receive extra tax credits of up to 25% of the income tax withheld from the retained employees.</p><p>The state will also offer a credit worth 10% of the training costs for new employees.</p><p>“Illinois continues to prove that it’s the right place for companies that want to grow and compete globally,” Pritzker said in the release. “Mars Snacking’s decision to expand its footprint in Chicago reflects the strength of our state’s workforce, infrastructure and business environment. We’re proud that a company with such an iconic portfolio is building on its history here, creating opportunity for Illinois families and strengthening our economy.”</p>
entries.author: Mariah Rush

entries.title: Bernie Bluestein, one of three remaining members of WWII ‘Ghost Army,’ dies at 102
entries.summary: <p>For about 50 years, what Bernie Bluestein did during World War II remained classified. If you asked, he might open an old cigar box in which he kept a piece of shrapnel, his dog tags, a swastika patch and a pocket knife.</p><p>But later, even after the U.S. government lifted the veil of secrecy, the Hoffman Estates man rarely spoke about his part in one of the most audacious missions of the war — as a foot soldier in the “Ghost Army.”</p><p>“He didn’t feel like he was a hero. He didn’t brag. He was just very humble,” said his son, Keith Bluestein, 70, who lives in Buffalo Grove.</p><p>Bernie Bluestein died this week at a nursing home in Wheeling, his son said. He was 102.</p><p>And he died acknowledging, reluctantly, that his efforts were indeed heroic, likely saving thousands of lives during the war — a fact stamped indelibly into history, when Mr. Bluestein and two other surviving Ghost Army veterans traveled to Washington, D.C. in 2024 to receive the Congressional Gold Medal.</p><p>“I’m happy about all this glory, but it’s not me,” Mr. Bluestein <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2024/2/9/24059597/ghost-army-bernie-bluestein-wwii-congressional-gold-medal-world-war-ii" target="_blank" ><u>told the Chicago Sun-Times</u></a> on the eve of the Washington D.C. trip. “I grew up in a poor family. … I’m not the kind of guy who goes and blows his horn.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>U.S. Speaker of the House Rep. Mike Johnson (R-LA) presents the Congressional Gold Medal to veteran of the Ghost Army Bernie Bluestein as fellow Ghost Army veteran Seymour Nussenbaum attends the event during a presentation ceremony at the Emancipation Hall of the Capitol Visitor Center on March 21, 2024 on Capitol Hill in Washington, DC. The Congressional Gold Medal ceremony was held to honor the 23rd Headquarters Special Troops and the 3133rd Signal Services Company, known collectively as the Ghost Army. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Alex Wong/Getty</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Deception is as old as warfare itself. But the 23rd Headquarters Special Troops and the 3133rd Signal Company Special were unusual in that they were two mobile, multimedia units devoted entirely to the art of deception — units brought together toward the end of the war.</p><p>All sworn to secrecy, the soldiers weren’t allowed to talk about the mission with loved ones or even other soldiers.</p><p>Props included inflatable tanks, trucks and howitzers, misleading radio communications and speakers blasting the sounds of fake convoys on the move.</p><p>Their aim was to draw the Nazis’ attention away from the movements of the real Allied forces.</p><p>The Ghost Army units were pulled into about two dozen military operations.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d90000" name="image-d90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
