1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/70221c5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/400x224+0+188/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdd%2F65%2F0dbacbcb47ed92afd889e1b9b46c%2Fghostarmy-021124-14.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5e4bec7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/400x224+0+188/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdd%2F65%2F0dbacbcb47ed92afd889e1b9b46c%2Fghostarmy-021124-14.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bernie Bluestein, as a soldier. | </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Bernie Bluestein</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Their last — convincing the Germans that an Allied Rhine River crossing would occur farther south than actually planned — was perhaps the most successful. The real crossing elsewhere ended up taking the Germans by surprise, likely saving thousands of lives, according to historians and military experts.</p><p>Given that their job was to draw enemy fire, casualties were relatively light: three soldiers killed in action and approximately 30 wounded by artillery fire.</p><p>That piece of shrapnel that Mr. Bluestein kept in a cigar box? A two-inch chunk that landed between his legs during a German bombing raid soon after he got to France.</p><p>“After it was all over, I said, ‘What did I get myself into?’” Mr. Bluestein said back in 2024. “Nobody explained it to us. When you’re in the army, you listen to the person above you.”</p><p>Mr. Bluestein, who was a 19-year-old art student in Cleveland when he was drafted, later moved to Chicago and worked as an industrial designer for Sunbeam, Zenith and other companies.</p><p>Rick Beyer, a Chicago author and filmmaker who has spent much of the past two decades telling the Ghost Army’s stories, led the effort to have the veterans recognized with Congressional Gold Medal.</p><p>Beyer had traveled with Mr. Bluestein to some of the World War II battle sites; he also visited Mr. Bluestein regularly during the last six months of his life.</p><p>“This is a tremendous passing in my life,” Beyer said. “These guys were such heroes … Bernie and I said ‘I love you’ many times.”</p><p>Said Mr. Bernstein’s son: “I’m proud of his service. I will make sure that his legacy and the legacy of the Ghost Army will never be forgotten.”</p><p>Survivors include a daughter, Aleyce Lacy of Braselton, Ga., and three grandchildren.</p><p>Service arrangements are pending and are expected to be held in Cleveland.</p>
entries.author: Stefano Esposito

entries.title: Humbold Park shooting leaves man, 26, critically injured
entries.summary:
A 26-year-old man was shot and critically wounded Thursday morning in the Humboldt Park neighborhood.

The man was standing outside in the 3600 block of West Grand Avenue when a gunman jumped from a vehicle and opened fire at the 26-year-old, hitting him multiple times, police said.

He was taken to Stroger Hospital in critical condition.

The shooter fled in an unknown direction.

Area 4 Detectives are investigating.
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Man shot last year in Roseland dies
entries.summary: <p>A Thursday autopsy found that a 65-year-old man who was shot last year in the Roseland neighborhood died this week of complications from multiple gunshot wounds and his death was ruled a homicide.</p><p>Claude Dixon, 65, was shot on Nov. 20 about 6:20 a.m. at 68 W. 112th Place, according to the Cook County medical examiner's office. </p><p>Dixon was pronounced dead Wednesday at 5:17 a.m. at Kindred North Hospital and an autopsy Thursday determined he died of complications from multiple gunshot wounds, according to the medical examiner's office. </p><p>The death of Dixon, who lived in the Roseland neighborhood, was ruled a homicide. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Jeff Blashill trying to help Blackhawks navigate lack of practice time
entries.summary: <p>PHILADELPHIA — During the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>' stretch of 21 games in 40 days between Christmas and the Olympics, coach Jeff Blashill tried to give his players as many breaks as possible, but the young roster still got worn down by the gantlet.</p><p>At that time, Blashill was already thinking ahead to a spring gantlet, as well. He mentioned on Dec. 27 that March would be an even "harder stretch" schedule-wise.</p><p>That has proven accurate. The Hawks are currently in the middle of a stretch of seven games in 11 days, which is part of a larger stretch of 12 games in 21 days.</p><p>Every off-day must be cherished when there are so few, which means the Hawks haven't gotten to practice (aside from brief morning skates) since March 16 — and have practiced only twice since the March 6 trade deadline. </p><p>Their next practice opportunity won't be until next Monday, March 30, at the earliest. And even that might not be the wisest idea, since it falls during a stretch of four games in four different time zones — moving one zone west each game — in a seven-day span.</p><p>"It’s a crazy amount of games, so you do the guys a disservice if you practice and they have zero energy," Blashill said last weekend. "And sometimes you do your team a disservice if you don’t practice at all. So it’s a balance. But right now, the balance has really shifted toward playing. </p><p>"We’re going to have to learn on video. And I’ll say this: Our team has done a pretty good job of learning on video. Usually when we show them stuff, they go out and apply it as good as any team I’ve been around."</p><p>Newly arrived rookie forwards Anton Frondell and Sacha Boisvert are most disadvantaged by the lack of practice time. They aren't getting much on-ice time to learn their teammates' tendencies and body language or rehearse the Hawks' systems.</p><h3><b>Schaefer show</b></h3><p>Islanders rookie phenom Matthew Schaefer, this year's runaway Calder Trophy winner-to-be, looked extremely good <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/24/blackhawks-islanders-anton-frondell-nhl-debut-nick-lardis" target="_blank" >Tuesday against the Hawks</a>, and they noticed.</p><p>Schaefer logged a career-high 31:59 of ice time (as in more than half of the 60-minute game) and recorded 17 shot attempts, including eight on goal. He has 22 goals and 30 assists in 72 games as an 18-year-old defenseman — an unbelievable stat line.</p><p>"He's a guy that finds ways to get pucks to the net a lot," Ryan Donato said Thursday. "And his engine is pretty crazy. It's nonstop, getting up and down the ice. With those type of guys, you just try to limit [them].</p><p>"It is fun to watch that guy play, I'll tell you that. Not fun when you're on the ice, but when you're watching him from afar."</p><h3><b>Soderblom’s stat</b></h3><p>Although Hawks goalie Arvid Soderblom has overall struggled this season (.883 save percentage in 22 appearances), he has also delivered some spectacular performances behind a leaky defense.</p><p>He has made 44 or more saves in each of his last two starts (against the Avalanche and Islanders) and now has four games with 44-plus saves this season.</p><p>Entering Thursday, only eight other NHL goalies had any games with 44-plus saves this season, and none had more than one.</p><p>"I've been getting used to it here over the years," Soderblom said Tuesday. "Even though you [face] a lot of shots and sometimes you might might be a little tired, then it's even more important to just stay in there and keep battling and just have the focus up all the way."</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Jedixson Paez goes from High-A to Opening Day in White Sox' bullpen
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — A smile crept across the face of <b>Jedixson Páez</b> Thursday as he walked onto the diamond at American Family Field Thursday, and there was no wiping it off.</p><p>Six months removed from his last appearance at High-A Greenville in the Red Sox organization, Páez arrived in Milwaukee as a big leaguer cracking the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>’ Opening Day roster.</p><p>Manager <b>Will Venable </b>delivered the news to the 6-1 right-hander that he’d achieved his boyhood dream. Páez’s first call went to his parents in Venezuela.</p><p>“They were screaming and they were very, very happy,“ Páez said through translator <b>Billy Russo</b>. “It was incredible. You’ve been dreaming about this moment your whole life and when it happens, you can’t believe it.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">the moment Jedixson found out the news 🥹 <a href="https://t.co/RmDNlWNSvg">pic.twitter.com/RmDNlWNSvg</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2037172049576185881?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 26, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Plucked by the White Sox in the Rule 5 Draft, Páez had a 2.79 ERA while limited to seven starts last year with a calf injury. Despite the inexperience, Venable said he expects Páez to fill an important long-relief role with his mid-90s fastball and a solid changeup.</p><p>“Just a calm dude, a guy that kind of feels like he's been doing it for a while,” Venable said, praising the even-keeled demeanor Páez showed in spring training. “It never really looked like it was too fast for him, which is a good indicator here as we know it'll speed up at this level.”</p><p>The Sox are hoping to get more from the Rule 5 well that produced rookie All-Star <b>Shane Smith</b>, who kicked off his second season as the Opening Day starter, and Swiss-army righty <b>Mike Vasil</b>, who dazzled in a slew of roles out of the bullpen last year. Tommy John surgery <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/17/white-sox-mike-vasil-to-undergo-season-ending-tommy-john-surgery" target="_blank" ><u>has wiped out Vasil’s sophomore showing</u></a>.</p><p>“To replicate that is not the easiest thing in the world. We were fortunate to be able to do it last year, but [Páez] is going to get a chance, and he's more than capable of doing it” general manager <b>Chris Getz</b> said. “We'll see how it unfolds.”</p><h3><b>Movin’ Mune</b></h3><p>Japanese slugger <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> made his MLB debut batting sixth, to the chagrin of some Sox fans who would’ve liked to see the powerful first baseman closer to the heart of the order.</p><p>Venable cited a strategic benefit to separating Murakami in the order from fellow left-handed bats <b>Colson Montgomery</b>, who batted second, and <b>Andrew Benintendi</b>, who hit fourth.</p><p>“He's going to move around to the lineup. You could see him anywhere from four to seven,” Venable said of Murakami. “That just kind of makes it a little tougher on the opposition to lay a lefty [reliever] in there.”</p><p>Murakami wasn’t concerned about it ahead of his first start in a Sox uniform.</p><p>“Anything I can do to contribute to the win for this team is what I will do at the end,” the 26-year-old <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/17/white-sox-announce-rotation-following-opening-day-starter-shane-smith" target="_blank" ><u>Nippon Professional Baseball legend</u></a> said through translator <b>Kenzo Yagi</b>.</p><h3><b>Teelin’ alright</b></h3><p>Second-year catcher <b>Kyle Teel</b> was disappointed to miss what would’ve been his first Opening Day, but he said he’s on track track to start his season midway through April as he recovers from the hamstring injury he suffered <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/11/white-sox-catcher-kyle-teel-suffers-grade-2-right-hamstring-strain-in-team-italys-world-baseball-classic-win" target="_blank" ><u>playing for Italy in the World Baseball Classic</u></a>. Teel suited up for pre-game workouts.</p><p>“He is recovering well. We're not going to push it just because we know that's not an injury that you want to get overly aggressive on, but he is responding well,” Getz said of one of his two prized backstops.</p><p><b>Edgar Quero</b> figures to carry the load for the first few weeks of the season.</p><p></p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, count=9779, pullQuoteIcon=[{image=null, narrowImage=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=null}], relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, items=[{type=Image, category=[{items=[White Sox]}], _styledTemplate=null, media=[{narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/1c/a3/c1e72aa4484d9268f3c704f1b754/getz32526.jpg, width=4032, height=3024}, image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/1c/a3/c1e72aa4484d9268f3c704f1b754/getz32526.jpg, width=4032, height=3024}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=White Sox general manager Chris Getz speaks to reporters at American Family Field in Milwaukee on Wednesday.}], publishDate=March 25, 2026 07:12 PM, imageAlignment=null, categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox, isFeatured=false, wordCount=682, target=null, primaryMediaType=null, description=[{items=[After more than two years at the helm, Getz &amp; entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: With Bears in mind, Sanders bill would offer up sports teams to city before 'billionaire owners' try to move
entries.summary: <p>Sen. Bernie Sanders and Texas Rep. Greg Casar want communities to be able to buy a sports team before "billionaire owners" try to move it, very pointedly bashing the Chicago Bears' effort to relocate to Indiana which Casar repeatedly likened to "negotiating with a gun" to the head.</p><p>While it's typically never a good thing for the Chicago Bears to be compared to the Green Bay Packers, a new bill would allow the Bears, and any other sports team, to be offered up for sale one year before sports team owners can move it to another state or metropolitan area. The legislation would lift a prohibition on teams being publicly owned, like the Packers are. The Wisconsin team is a non profit franchise in the NFL, with more than 538,000 shareholders. </p><p>Casar even went so far to say that "this is a keep-the-Bears-in-Chicago bill" during a Washington press conference on Thursday. </p><p>The Home Team Act would require an owner to announce that it wants to relocate a year prior, giving the community, not-for-profits or private investors a chance to buy it at "fair market value," including through the community ownership model of the Packers. Franchise owners would be penalized if they don't comply with the one-year notice.</p><p>"There is a right of first refusal to the local community, to community nonprofits and partnerships and for ultimately, if it's just a wealthy family that wants to keep it, they get a shot at it, too," Casar said. "But you could imagine a world where the city has a stake in the team, alongside other partners."</p><p>If no one wants to buy the team and keep it in the home city, owners can then move it. Casara called it a way for fans and taxpayers to "finally get a fair deal."</p><p>"Even when teams don't actually move, the threat of moving sets off a race to the bottom. Billionaire owners pit taxpayers against one another and then extort the government for billions of dollars," Casar said.</p><p>"We see that right now in Chicago, where Indiana taxpayers are now put in a competition against Illinois taxpayers to subsidize a team worth $8 billion," he said. "Before you move a team away from the fans who have been rooting for it their whole life, you have to give them a chance to buy it and keep it."</p><p>Casar said it's possible to get "much better deals if you aren't negotiating with a gun to your head, like we're seeing right now in Illinois." </p><p>Sanders said the departure of the Dodgers from Brooklyn, New York had a very negative impact on the state, and he called the new legislation "emotional."</p><p>"It's a big deal, and we should not let the greed of a handful of billionaires make it hard for working-class families to enjoy their local teams," Sanders said.</p><p>Indiana state legislators last month advanced legislation <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/19/chicago-bears-stadium-hammond-indiana-move" target="_blank" >creating a stadium finance authority that would help the Bears build a dome in Hammond</a>, a move that the team called "the most meaningful step forward" in a dramatic saga that has stretched on for more than three years. It was also a step taken by the Bears that frustrated Illinois officials, who are trying to craft a deal to keep the Bears in Arlington Heights, a destination that Gov. JB Pritzker and other Illinois Democrats say remains within reach. </p><p>A partial plan to help the NFL franchise move to Arlington Heights moved out of an Illinois House committee in late February. But its <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/26/chicago-bears-property-tax-incentives-illinois-house-city-opposition-arlington-heights-proposed-stadium" target="_blank" >legislative prospects are in question</a> after City Hall withheld support and kept pushing for at least $630 million in infrastructure and Soldier Field renovation concessions.</p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: Americans, once 'suffocated by smog, poisoned by water,' face return to bad old days
entries.summary: <p>In 1889, the American correspondent for the Allahabad Pioneer arrived on the shores of Lake Michigan and was momentarily impressed.</p><p>“I have struck a city—a real city—and they call it Chicago,” Rudyard Kipling, 23, informed his readers in northern India. "The other places do not count."</p><p>But initial approval evaporated as he looked around, noting: "Its water is the water of the Hughli," — a branch of the Ganges in West Bengal famous for its pollution, "and its air is dirt."</p><p>Unregulated industry — enormous stockyards dumping into the canals, making them, in Kipling's words, "black as ink, and filled with untold abominations," smokestacks belching filth — will do that. Today when we call Chicagoans "gritty" we are speaking about toughness; 137 years ago, it meant they were coated in coal dust.</p><p>What changed? Well, conscientious businesses, concerned about the effect pollution was having on the quality of life of their neighbors, took it upon themselves to clean up their acts and ...</p><p>Ha-ha, just kidding. Early April Fool's. No, of course, business, then and now, only cares about short-term profits. But government forced them to act in a socially-responsible fashion, setting health standards and limiting pollution. Only then did city dwellers breathe easier, and "grit" could fade into colorful metaphor.</p><p>I was reminded of this flipping open the latest edition of the New England Journal of Medicine and coming upon <a class="Link" href="https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMp2514370?query=TOC" target="_blank" >“The Dismantling of Environmental Protections — A Grave Threat to America’s Health” </a>by a pair of Harvard doctors, Adam W. Gaffney and David Himmelstein, joined by three other health experts.</p><p>They start with another once notoriously dirty city — Cleveland — and the 1969 combustion of its Cuyahoga River, so polluted it caught fire, "sparking national attention to environmental degradation."</p><p>A president not famous for his selflessness, but Cincinattus compared to our current commander-in-chief, took decisive action:</p><p>"In his State of the Union address seven months later, President Richard Nixon lamented that Americans were being 'suffocated by smog, poisoned by water' and proclaimed that clean air and water should 'be the birthright of every American.' At Nixon’s urging, Congress established the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and passed the Clean Air Act (CAA) with bipartisan support. Air-quality upgrades mandated by that act and enforced by the EPA are among the most effective health interventions of the past half-century, having reduced air pollution by 75% in the United States and saved at least 200,000 lives per year."</p><p>Now our birthright is being taken away — it isn't just voting. Our country is in full retreat regarding the environment.</p><p>"Yet during the first administration of President Donald Trump, nearly 100 environmental and occupational protections, including air-quality safeguards, were rescinded," they write. "Although many of those rescissions were delayed by litigation or reversed by President Joe Biden, they inflicted considerable harm on Americans’ health. The second Trump administration’s actions have been even more aggressive, portending greater harm."</p><p>Who benefits? The bottom line of big businesses receiving these breaks, often in return for big checks cut to you-know-who. Who will be hurt? You, your children, the neighborhood you live in, your city and country.</p><p>From abandonment of the Paris Accords fighting climate change, to loosening automobile emissions standards, to undermining electric cars, the Trump administration makes sure every day is Christmas for corporate polluters, not to forget their wholesale effort to scrap alternate forms of energy, with particular hostility toward wind — the Trump administration paid a French company $1 billion not to build wind farms in the Atlantic. </p><p>"This administration’s actions will undo the work of generations," the authors warn, urging, "We health professionals must call urgent attention to this silent but deadly assault on Americans’ health, work with broad coalitions to halt it, and ultimately rebuild the agencies, protections, and shared sense of trust and responsibility that have given us clean air and water and enabled us and our children to live longer, healthier lives."</p><p>We can't leave Kipling — future author of "The Jungle Book" and Rod Blagojevich's favorite author — without noting his other complaints. Chicagoans speak only of business, which to the young poet was “like listening to a child babble of its hoard of shells.” He scanned the newspapers, and immediately noticed Chicago’s tussle with another city over hosting a trade show, and never will the World’s Columbian Exposition receive quite the casual backhand that Kipling offered, reducing the struggle to “some sort of dispute between New York and Chicago as to which town should be given an exhibition of products.”</p><p>The Allahabad Pioneer, by the way, still exists, as The Pioneer, published in 10 Indian cities. Kipling wasn't their only literary star: Winston Churchill was a war correspondent, and in 1937 the paper tried to put George Orwell on staff.</p>
entries.author: Neil Steinberg

entries.title: Sky’s free-agency path runs through the next tier, not the stars
entries.summary: <p>Now that the WNBA’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/wnba/2026/03/18/finally-a-deal-wnba-and-players-reach-cba-agreement" >new collective-bargaining agreement</a> has been ratified, teams can finally start building their rosters.</p><p>During a hectic couple of weeks in April, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky" target="_blank" >Sky</a> will attempt to add talent around returners Angel Reese, Kamilla Cardoso and Courtney Vandersloot.</p><p>Broadcaster Ryan Ruocco is calling it a “women’s basketball cyclone” of activity.</p><p>A key component of that cyclone is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/wnba/2026/03/24/wnba-owners-ratify-collective-bargaining-agreement-draft-free-agency" >free agency</a>. Every veteran but two is available to be signed, and the Sky need help at almost every position. They need a point guard to play behind Vandersloot. They need an athletic wing to bolster their defense.</p><p>What would really break things open, though, is signing a top-of-the-line, drop-20-a-game kind of shooting guard.</p><p>That guard could be the closer when they need a bucket late in games.</p><p>That guard would draw attention away from Reese and Cardoso.</p><p>That guard would help them play at a faster pace.</p><p>It’s rare to see this many players of that nature on the market.</p><p>There are the purest examples of this archetype: Jackie Young and Allisha Gray.</p><p>There are the Kelseys: Mitchell and Plum.</p><p>And the long-range assassins: Sabrina Ionescu and Arike Ogunbowale.</p><p>So what kind of pitch could the Sky — coming off two losing seasons — make to woo a star like this?</p><p>Let’s start with Jackie Young.</p><p>Chicago would represent a return to the Midwest, where Young is from, and a reunion with her former coach, Tyler Marsh. The two bonded in Las Vegas, where Marsh helped transform Young’s 3-point shooting as an assistant on Becky Hammon’s staff.</p><p>At a recent USA Basketball training camp, Young told the Sun-Times the two still keep in touch.</p><p>But she stopped short of revealing whether she’d like to play for him again.</p><p>“That’s my guy,” she said, laughing. “That’s all I’ll say.”</p><p>Go ahead, Sky fans. Take a minute to enjoy the fantasy. All those dimes Young could receive from Sloot, all the transition takes and tough shots in the lane.</p><p>But then you have to let it go.</p><p>Because it would take a miracle for the Sky to pry Young away from Las Vegas, where she just won a championship and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/wnba/2025/10/11/the-wnba-season-ends-with-aja-wilsons-tambourine-and-the-commissioner-walking-the-plank" >helped build a dynasty.</a></p><p>That’s the case with most players in this category: they’re either coming off a deep playoff run (Mitchell) or have expressed a commitment to their current team (Ionescu).</p><p>And the kicker is that if any of these players do want to leave, their current teams can still use the core designation to ensure they get a return. The only way to land a “cored” player would be through a sign-and-trade.</p><p>So the best opportunity on the market is with the secondary stars: free agents like Skylar Diggins, Kayla McBride, Courtney Williams and Jordin Canada.</p><p>The problem is that the Sky still don’t have a clear path to contention. That’s a tough sell to players who will have their pick of 15 teams.</p><p>Where the Sky might be able to do damage is with the next tier of players. Call them bubble players — ones who could either start or come off the bench depending on the team.</p><p>Natisha Hiedeman, Ty Harris and Sami Whitcomb fit into this category.</p><p>The new CBA gives the Sky an opportunity here. Contending teams will now have to spend less on their depth, given that stars take up a larger share of the salary cap.</p><p>That opens the door for the Sky to lure these bubble players away. They can offer something the superteams can’t: a fat paycheck and a starting role.</p><p>Loading up on bubble players could help create a much better supporting cast around Reese, Cardoso and Vandersloot than they had last year.</p><p>This would make the team easier on the eyes. Maybe even lift them a few spots in the standings. But they’d still need to add a star to become a real postseason threat.</p><h3><b>Where does two-time All-Star Ariel Atkins fit?</b></h3><p>The Sky’s best guard last season was Ariel Atkins. She averaged 13.1 points and 3.6 assists per game but <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-sky/2025/06/17/ariel-atkins-still-struggling-to-go-alpha-mode-in-skys-loss-to-the-mystics" >never looked fully comfortable as the primary scoring option.</a></p><p>She also didn’t play as well as the No. 3 pick in the 2025 draft, instant All-Star Sonia Citron. The Sky traded that pick for Atkins, who was on a one-year deal and has not indicated whether she wants to return.</p><p>For good reason, the trade caused almost as much anxiety among fans as when the Sky gave up a truckload of draft capital for guard Marina Mabrey in 2023.</p><p>The saving grace here is the core tag. If Atkins decides to leave, the Sky can core her to ensure they get some draft capital back in a sign-and-trade.</p><p>Just don’t expect a full refund (i.e., the No. 3 pick in the 2026 draft), given that the Sky likely overpaid in the first place.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Alissa Hirsh

entries.title: 'Huge Trump supporter' from Lake County convicted of threatening Trump
entries.summary: <p>Federal jurors on Thursday convicted a Winthrop Harbor man of threatening<b> </b>judges, doctors, lawyers, police and President Donald Trump — even though the man’s lawyer told them “this is the biggest Trumper you’re going to find.”</p><p>Trent<b> </b>Schneider, 58, now faces up to five years<b> </b>in prison over a video he posted online<b> </b>last fall, in which he railed against people who had done him wrong and ultimately dropped the president’s name. He said, “people like me have suffered real f---ing crimes from f---ing judges, doctors, lawyers, police. They all should be killed.”</p><p>“I think it’s time,” Schneider continued. “I’ve waited long enough. I’m going to get some guns. I know where I can get a lot of f---ing guns and I am going to take care of business myself. I’m tired of all you f---ing frauds. People need to f---ing die and people are going to die.”</p><p>Finally, he added, “f--- all of you, especially you, Trump. You should be executed.”</p><p>U.S. District Judge Sharon Johnson Coleman did not, however, commit Thursday<b> </b>to keeping Schneider in custody while he awaits sentencing.<b> </b>Schneider already has been held since his arrest<b> </b>in November, records show. Lawyers are due back in court on the case Tuesday.</p><p>Schneider could be seen subtly shaking his head Thursday as the verdict was read. It came down in the morning, after jurors on Wednesday had heard testimony from Schneider and then closing arguments.</p><p>Assistant U.S. Attorney Paige<b> </b>Nutini<b> </b>explained during her argument how Schneider’s threat came against the backdrop of foreclosure proceedings against Schneider in Lake County. His home was ultimately set for auction Nov. 4, according to a criminal complaint.</p><p>Schneider was arrested over his video one day earlier, records show.</p><p>“You know that it was a true threat because you have eyes, you have ears, and you have common sense,” Nutini told the jury. “You can see that the defendant’s words are clearly and unmistakably an intent to commit violence. You can hear the seriousness and the anger with which he says his words.”</p><p>Defense attorney Michael Leonard denied Schneider’s comments amounted to a true threat, questioning whether Schneider really intended “to assassinate thousands to millions of people” by saying “all”<b> </b>judges, doctors, lawyers and police should be killed.</p><p>Not only that, but Leonard said Schneider is “a huge Trump supporter.”<b> </b>Videos shown to the jury depicted Schneider wearing a black baseball cap featuring a Punisher-style skull with Trump hair.</p><p>“This is the biggest Trumper you’re going to find, maybe, in this room,” Leonard said during his closing argument.</p><p>Regardless, Leonard said Schneider never took action to follow through on any threat.</p><p>After the verdict, Leonard told the Sun-Times he was “surprised”<b> </b>by the verdict but “we think we have a strong basis for appeal.” The issue, he said, was not with the jury.</p><p>“It’s with how the law defines what’s called a ‘true threat,’” he said.</p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Lincoln items once destined for state museum sell as part of $1.3 million auction
entries.summary: <p>Nearly 200 artifacts mostly linked to Abraham Lincoln sold for more than $1 million at a Chicago auction Thursday, including some items originally purchased for display at the state-run Lincoln presidential museum in Springfield.</p><p>The sale by Freeman’s is believed to have included pieces of a $23 million purchase in 2007 by a foundation originally set up to acquire one-of-a-kind Lincoln relics for the Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library and Museum.</p><p>One of those that appeared to be from that acquisition from West Coast Lincoln aficionado Louise Taper was a folding framed portrait of Abraham Lincoln’s assassin John Wilkes Booth. Inside the frame were a few loose strands of dark hair purportedly plucked from Booth’s head after he was gunned down at Garrett’s Farm on April 26, 1865.</p><p>That piece sold for $7,000, which was below the auction house’s pre-auction price estimate of between $10,000 and $15,000.</p><p>Other items linked to Taper was a bust of Lincoln biographer Carl Sandburg once owned by Marilyn Monroe that sold for $1,300. That fell in the range of Freeman's pre-auction price estimate of between $1,000 and $2,000.</p><p>Another piece that appeared to come from Taper’s collection was a bust of former Union Army General and 18th President Ulysses Grant. It fetched $2,000, which was also in line with Freeman’s pre-auction estimate.</p><p>A rare 34-star eagle parade flag with the names of Lincoln and Vice President Andrew Johnson fetched the most money. The $220,000 item, which was not affiliated with Taper's collection, was the only one to surpass a $100,000 bid.</p><p>The owner of the Taper items, the Lincoln Presidential Foundation, would not publicly say if it was selling off more of its Lincoln artifacts to pay off remaining debt from the 2007 purchase.</p><p>Last May, the foundation funneled several one-of-a-kind Lincoln artifacts through Freeman’s for that purpose, including a set of blood-stained gloves that were in Lincoln’s coat pocket the night Booth assassinated him at Ford’s Theater. Also included was Lincoln’s earliest known handwriting sample from 1824.</p><p>Originally, the foundation and presidential museum were partners in the mission to acquire Lincoln artifacts for the public to see in Springfield, the 16th president’s hometown. Whatever got acquired eventually was to have been turned over to the state for permanent display in the museum.</p><p>But relations turned sour between the two entities, driven partly by acrimony surrounding the inability to authenticate a one-time cornerstone of the Taper acquisition, a stovepipe hat that purportedly belonged to Lincoln.</p>
entries.author: Sam Naftzger | WBEZ

entries.title: Cubs to extend second baseman Nico Hoerner
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/cubs" >Cubs</a> reached an agreement to extend second baseman Nico Hoerner following Thursday's 10-4 loss to the Nationals on Opening Day, a source confirmed to the Sun-Times. </p><p>Hoerner was set to reach free agency following the 2026 season and was going to be one of the premier names on the market. Hoerner batted .297/.345/.394 with a .739 OPS last season and earned a Gold Glove. He joins center fielder Pete Crow-Armstrong, who signed a six-year, $115 million extension , as Cubs to reach long-term agreements. </p><p>Hoerner debuted in 2019 as an emergency call-up for an injured Javier Baez. He played a bit role in the 2020-21 season before establishing himself as a major-league player in 2022, posting a 4.2-WAR season and batting .281/.327/.410. </p><p>As president of baseball operations Jed Hoyer dismantled the 2016 World Series core, Hoerner grew as a dependable and respected voice in the Cubs' clubhouse. Hoerner said before Thursday' s game he wanted to focus on the game and not his contract status, but the deal was reportedly agreed to after the game. He now joins Crow-Armstrong, Dansby Swanson and Alex Bregman as foundational players with guaranteed contracts that last past the 2027 season. </p><p></p><bsp-listcarousel class="PageListE" entries.title: Logistics firm TransLoop opens River North office with hiring push planned
entries.summary: <p>Freight brokerage TransLoop held a grand opening Thursday for its new Chicago headquarters, more than doubling its footprint in the city.</p><p>The company leased nearly 45,000 square feet of office space at 350 N. Orleans St. in River North. It has been on a hot growth trajectory since launching in 2019. </p><p>TransLoop planted its roots in a 200-square-foot office in River North. It continued to grow before <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2022/10/28/23429028/transloop-hires-staff-moves-chicago-headquarters" >moving to 1 S. Wacker Drive</a> in 2022, where it tripled the company’s footprint to 17,000 square feet. </p><p>“Our business exploded, and then we outgrew that,” TransLoop CEO and founder Nick Reasoner said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0b33057/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6503x3650+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F15%2Fde40d6c44238b7210260d3e28639%2Ftransloop-260327-8.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/deaf593/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6503x3650+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F15%2Fde40d6c44238b7210260d3e28639%2Ftransloop-260327-8.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/048f7ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6503x3650+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F15%2Fde40d6c44238b7210260d3e28639%2Ftransloop-260327-8.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Scott Houdek, TransLoop's director of financial operations (left); Nicholas Reasoner, founder and CEO of TransLoop; Chris Sujka, director of sales; and Brien Maloney, director of safety and compliance, at the logistics company's new River North office." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/048f7ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6503x3650+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F15%2Fde40d6c44238b7210260d3e28639%2Ftransloop-260327-8.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d3b604d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6503x3650+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F15%2Fde40d6c44238b7210260d3e28639%2Ftransloop-260327-8.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/048f7ab/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6503x3650+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F92%2F15%2Fde40d6c44238b7210260d3e28639%2Ftransloop-260327-8.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Scott Houdek, TransLoop’s director of financial operations (from left); Nick Reasoner, founder and CEO of TransLoop; Chris Sujka, director of sales; and Brien Maloney, director of safety and compliance, at the logistics company’s new River North office.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Reasoner said he never would have imagined the brokerage would grow to its current size, which he credits to the growth of the logistics market.</p><p>“There's just a ton of opportunity and market share to grab from competitors that aren't servicing their customers properly,” Reasoner said. “We've done a really good job of getting in with customers and being ethical and being transparent, finding new initiatives for shippers to bring us revenue. I think other people are kind of sleeping at the wheel, in a sense, and we're just very aggressive.”</p><p>Its 44,457-square-foot office on the ninth floor includes a kitchen, huddle rooms, phone rooms and desks for nearly 300 employees. TransLoop, which moved in last month, is subleasing the space from research company Gartner, according to CBRE, which represented TransLoop. And it shares the floor with a co-working lounge.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-120000" name="image-120000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f607c87/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6437x3613+0+48/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F47%2F6568b7ab4772b6da4a65dbcf6460%2Ftransloop-260327-5.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a800a66/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6437x3613+0+48/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F47%2F6568b7ab4772b6da4a65dbcf6460%2Ftransloop-260327-5.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c2a423/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6437x3613+0+48/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F47%2F6568b7ab4772b6da4a65dbcf6460%2Ftransloop-260327-5.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The lobby of TransLoop’s new office in River North." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c2a423/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6437x3613+0+48/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F47%2F6568b7ab4772b6da4a65dbcf6460%2Ftransloop-260327-5.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a9d3392/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6437x3613+0+48/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F47%2F6568b7ab4772b6da4a65dbcf6460%2Ftransloop-260327-5.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9c2a423/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6437x3613+0+48/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F2a%2F47%2F6568b7ab4772b6da4a65dbcf6460%2Ftransloop-260327-5.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The lobby of TransLoop’s new office in River North.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>TransLoop plans to increase its Chicago head count to 250 over the next two years for a number of positions, including customer sales. Its headquarters employs more than 100.</p><p>Reasoner said he’s a “big supporter” of Chicago and wants the company to continue to grow in the city. It also has offices in Birmingham, Alabama, Denver, Fort Lauderdale, Florida, and Nashville.</p><p>“This area, especially with the Salesforce Tower and Merch Mart, there's no better business area,” Reasoner said. “Wacker Drive is the business center, I feel like, in Chicago.”</p><p>TransLoop was drawn to 350 N. Orleans St. for its clean fully furnished space, allowing the company to avoid the headache of buying and moving furniture. The Wolf Point location was also a big draw for its access to transit and food options, Reasoner said. Most employees are in the office five days a week, which the company said is key to building its corporate culture.</p><p>"This is just a very comfortable space for the quiet people, who want to pull away and work in a quiet environment, and the people that want to be on our very robust and loud sales floor," Reasoner said. "You can find a hole everywhere in our office."</p>
entries.author: Abby Miller

entries.title: Chase Meidroth, Munetaka Murakami mash — but Brewers smash White Sox’ Opening Day hopes 14-2
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — Hopes are rarely higher than they are before first pitch on Opening Day.</p><p>Chase Meidroth sent the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" ><u>White Sox</u></a>’ good vibes soaring to a new stratosphere Thursday with a 417-foot laser over the left-field wall at American Family Field, slugging the first Opening Day leadoff home run in franchise history — and stoking hope for a big step forward for his rebuilding squad.</p><p>But it didn’t take long for ace Jacob Misiorowski and last year’s NLCS-finalist Brewers to send the Sox crashing back down to earth, reminding them just how far they have to go to catch up with a contending franchise that seems to do all the little things right.</p><p>Big ones, too, as Misiorowski recovered to mow down 11 Sox hitters over five innings — and that was barely the half it, as Sox starter Shane Smith couldn’t escape the second inning of a 14-2 clobbering for a downright ugly 2026 debut. </p><p>"We had 20 strikeouts on offense and 10 walks, so yeah, not good," second-year skipper Will Venable said after a deflating opener that was only momentarily salvaged by Japanese slugger Munetaka Murakami's first career hit, a 384-foot moonshot of a home run in garbage time. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">FIRST AT-BAT OF THE SEASON. LET'S GO! <a href="https://t.co/VLTOMnXipt">pic.twitter.com/VLTOMnXipt</a></p>— Chicago White Sox (@whitesox) <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox/status/2037232894146408810?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 26, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>A pair of walks and an interference call on Sox catcher Edgar Quero led to an RBI single for Brewers shortstop Joey Ortiz in the second inning, before William Contreras cleared a loaded set of bases with a double down the left-field line to end Smith’s day early. His first line following <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/23/sox-starter-shane-smith-roughed-up-in-first-outing-of-spring" >an All-Star rookie campaign</a> included four runs, three earned, off three hits and two walks to go with two strikeouts over 1 ⅔ innings.</p><p>"Not letting today define us as a team, or define me as a person," Smith said. "I think I made good pitches, I think I made bad pitches. The untimely walks have been a problem for me, even last year."</p><p>Milwaukee kept pounding <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/02/13/white-sox-bullpen-spring-training-seranthony-dominguez-jordan-hicks" >a revamped Sox bullpen</a>, getting a pair of runs each off free-agent arrival Sean Newcomb and returning veteran lefty Tyler Gilbert. The wheels fell off from there, with the Brewers tagging another offseason addition, Jordan Hicks, for three more. </p><p>Meanwhile, Sox bats mustered only a pair of infield singles to go with the two solo shots while striking out a staggering 20 times — the most for a nine-inning Opening Day game in MLB history. </p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/26/white-sox-win-total-2026-mlb-opening-day-chris-getz-will-venable-brewers" >Greenberg: Whether White Sox think they can or can’t take a real step forward in 2026, they’re right</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Not that there weren’t flashes of hope for the Sox’ future on display. Murakami showed impressive discipline in his first two plate appearances, working full counts for two walks against Misiorowski. </p><p>Murakami's first official at-bat didn’t come until the seventh inning, when he grounded out to first base. And he saved his real welcome for the ninth inning with his blast over right-field fence off Jake Woodford.</p><p>"I’m happy I was able to take the first step as a Major League Baseball player, but at the end of the day we lost the game," Murakami said through a translator. "I’ll try to prepare for the next game and try my best."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="ja" dir="ltr"><a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/%E3%83%9B%E3%83%AF%E3%82%A4%E3%83%88%E3%82%BD%E3%83%83%E3%82%AF%E3%82%B9?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#ホワイトソックス</a> 村上宗隆<br><br>村上がメジャー初ホームラン🔥<br>デビュー戦で特大ホームラン！<br><br>①四球<br>②四球<br>③一ゴロ<br>④ホームラン<a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/whitesox?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#whitesox</a> <a href="https://twitter.com/hashtag/murakami?src=hash&ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">#murakami</a> munetaka <a href="https://twitter.com/whitesox?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">@whitesox</a> <a href="https://t.co/zpViWmdlai">pic.twitter.com/zpViWmdlai</a></p>— 【MLB速報】放送地区【大谷速報】 (@MLB_comment) <a href="https://twitter.com/MLB_comment/status/2037276803300225265?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 26, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Quero made Sox history behind the dish with the first successful challenge in team history under MLB’s new Automated Ball-Strike Challenge System. It happened on the first batter the Sox faced this season, with Quero correcting home plate umpire Mark Wegner on a changeup from Smith to Brewers second baseman Brice Turang.</p><p>Quero got two more bad calls overturned as well, but was overruled on his fourth challenge.</p><p>It was a tough opening afternoon for other key components of general manager <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/25/white-sox-opening-day-chris-getz-rebuild-progress" target="_blank" ><u>Chris Getz’ organizational overhaul</u></a>. Shortstop Colson Montgomery and third baseman Miguel Vargas each went hitless with three strikeouts apiece.</p><p>"These guys, all last year, [after] tough games, came back and played with an edge," Venable said. "These guys will do it, for sure. We’ll be ready to go on Saturday."</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, pagination=[{body=[Next Page], href=?p=2, _template=/link/Link.hbs, contentId=null, pianoSectionSlug=null, target=null}], footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, showListNumbers=false, imagePositionHorizontal=null, rteBodyFont=font-3, headerBottomBorderColor=#111010, subTitle=null, stickyHeader=true, listTitleColor=#000000, tableCellBorderColor=#ccd1d5, count=9781, relatedListFont=font-1, hideAuthors=true, tertiaryTextColor=#6c6c6c, buttonHoverTextColor=#ffffff, tableFont=font-1, carouselAutoPlay=false, promoTitleFont=font-2, ctaButtonText=[], primaryTextColor=#2a2a2a, buttonTextColor=#ffffff, linkTextColor=#1a1a1a, hideTitles=false, footerLinkColor=#ffffff, items=[{type=Image, media=[{alt=White Sox pitcher Jedixson Paez tosses the ball to first base forcing out the Milwaukee Brewers' Luis Rengifo during the sixth inning of an Opening Day game on Thursday, March 26, 2026, in Milwaukee. entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Mayor Johnson's veto over freezing tipped minimum wage divides restaurant industry
entries.summary: <p>Some restaurant owners and servers say Chicago’s hotly debated law to increase the tipped minimum wage harms workers and the industry, following Mayor Brandon Johnson's <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/25/brandon-johnson-veto-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants" target="_blank" >veto</a> of the City Council’s vote to freeze tipped workers’ hourly pay. Supporters of the ordinance want better pay and conditions for workers.</p><p>Chicago’s law went into effect in 2024 to increase the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2024/07/01/chicago-tipped-workers-five-year-plan-raise-subminimum-wages-restaurants" >tipped minimum wage</a> to parity with the city’s standard minimum wage over five years.</p><p>“The recent backdoor effort to pause its implementation is not only harmful to tipped workers — especially Black, Brown and women workers — but also undermines the democratic process,” said Raeghn Draper, organizer with the Chicago Hospitality Accountability & Advocacy Database Project and bartender at Consignment Lounge near Logan Square. CHAAD is part of One Fair Wage, the advocacy group leading a national campaign to end the tipped minimum wage.</p><p>But Shanell Oliver, a server at Bronzeville Winery, makes much more with tips. During a 4 1/2 hour shift last Sunday, she made $425 in tips at the upscale South Side restaurant.</p><p>“No server is working to make the hourly wage,” she said. Someone could work at a fast-food restaurant to earn minimum wage, she pointed out.<b> </b></p><p>For Oliver, Chicago’s law increasing the tipped minimum wage amounts to only a few more dollars an hour. Tipped workers are currently paid a base wage of $12.62 an hour, compared to the city’s standard minimum wage of $16.60.</p><p>“Servers don’t work for minimum wage. They work for tips,” said Eric Williams, owner of Bronzeville Winery. “That’s not the battle to fight.”</p><p>Increasing minimum wages for tipped workers means rising fixed costs for restaurants. That drives some owners to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2025/05/23/chicago-restaurateurs-one-fair-wage-ordinance-tipped-workers" target="_blank" >cut staff</a> as well as their hours.</p><p>“You end up actually working fewer shifts and making less money,” said Oliver, a single mother of three who has worked as a server for two decades.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/25/brandon-johnson-veto-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants" >Mayor Johnson vetoes plan to freeze subminimum wage for tipped workers</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Since 2024, Bronzeville Winery has reduced its servers from about 10 to six, Williams said. The restaurant has also raised menu prices to offset additional higher costs due to ongoing inflation and tariffs.</p><p>Williams said in a rare case where tips don’t bring servers to the standard minimum wage threshold, many restaurant payroll systems automatically pay the base rate.</p><p>The ordinance doesn’t just hurt smaller restaurants like Bronzeville Winery, Williams said. It “will destroy restaurants in the city, especially the ones who hire Black employees. The Black restaurant owner is the one who bets on our neighborhoods, not outside special interest groups who come into our city, lobby and leave."</p><p>Instead of hiring servers, restaurants could shift to counter service or using technology such as apps and tablets.</p><p>They are also cutting opening hours and closing earlier, said Gina Barge-Farmer, owner of Wax Vinyl Bar and Ramen Shop near Noble Square. As a result, servers work shorter shifts and don’t earn as much. Wax Vinyl opened nearly two years ago and has also cut staff due to rising costs.</p><p>Wax Vinyl’s servers typically make more than $30 an hour. “My goal is to make my staff as much money as humanly possible,” Barge-Farmer said.</p><p>The controversy about the tipped minimum wage is “a misunderstanding of math,” she said. “We're on the hook for paying the minimum wage, irrespective.”</p><p>Under federal law, restaurant owners must pay tipped workers the local hourly minimum wage if their tips plus base salary don’t reach that minimum threshold. In other words, if servers earn less than the standard hourly minimum wage, restaurateurs must pay them the difference.</p><p>The federal Fair Labor Standards Act created this “tip credit” system and employers who violate the law are supposed to be penalized. But not all restaurants comply, which might be more often the case with smaller, less-established eateries.</p><p>Illinois Restaurant Association President Sam Toia has urged crackdowns on employers who don’t pay the guaranteed minimum wage rather than requiring Chicago restaurants to hike the tipped minimum wage.</p><p>Barge-Farmer believes Chicago’s restaurant scene is the best in the country. But she cautions anyone against opening a restaurant here.</p><p>“Don’t do it. It’s harder and harder to build anything sustainable,” she said. “One would think the city of Chicago would be more supportive. Instead, it’s death by a thousand cuts.”</p><p>Wax Vinyl operates on thin margins. “If it continues this way, I can’t say I want to continue. I’d be happy to close if it gets worse,” Barge-Farmer said.</p><p>The mayor “has very good intentions,” Oliver said. But “these policies make it difficult for other restaurants to go into the headwinds in under-resourced communities,” such as Bronzeville and South Shore, where she lives.</p><p>Williams said, “I understand the mayor is trying to raise wages for workers. But you have to get under the hood to understand how restaurants work.”</p><p>He pointed out that activists who originally spearheaded the campaign don’t live in the community. “We should be the ones who are listened to,” he said.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/18/city-council-freeze-subminimum-wage-phase-out-tipped-workers-restaurants-brandon-johnson" >City Council freezes pay for tipped workers at 76% of minimum wage to help struggling restaurants</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Whether White Sox think they can or can't take a real step forward in 2026, they're right
entries.summary: <p>MILWAUKEE — So, how many games should the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> win this season?</p><p>Sixty-plus? Seventy-plus? Dare I go on?</p><p>But the question itself is a fair one, even if Sox general manager Chris Getz and manager Will Venable still treat <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/25/white-sox-opening-day-chris-getz-rebuild-progress" target="_blank" >any discussion of total wins and losses</a> as a rhetorical third rail.</p><p>You just don’t go there with these guys, not even on Opening Day, unless mumbo-jumbo, hocus-pocus and argle-bargle happen to be three of your very favorite things.</p><p>“What’s really taking another meaningful step forward?” Getz said. “Whether that be in wins and losses, consistency of our competitiveness throughout the year [or] the confidence that grows within a clubhouse and for individual players at this level, you want to reflect back, once we get through this season, on what 2026 was for us [and have] a better idea of where we are in the development of this major league club and the organization as a whole.”</p><p>Once more, in terms that might enable the average yo-yo to glean something semi-meaningful?</p><p>“I do anticipate winning more games this year,” Getz went on. “But we don’t focus on win-loss record. We focus on winning the game that’s right in front of you.”</p><p>Venable, too, avoids getting specific when it comes to defining success in 2026, other than expressing his wishes for fewer mistakes and more consistent execution. I’m no baseball man, yet I’d be willing to bet no skipper in the history of the game has wished for the other way around.</p><p>“Just going out there every day and completing those things and executing those things,” he said. “We’ve got a very clear idea of what those things are on a daily basis, we have a plan in place to go out and execute them, and we’ve got to go execute them. We’re going to hold ourselves to that standard [and] that’s how we’re going to measure how this is going.”</p><p>There are “W” and “L” columns for that, just saying.</p><p>As the Brewers were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/03/26/white-sox-opening-day-loss-brewers-shane-smith-chase-meidroth" >steamrolling the Sox 14-2</a> in Game No. 1 of 162 for each team, it was impossible to watch without experiencing a sense of here-we-go-again. The Sox have lost a mind-numbing 324 times over the last three seasons. They’ve put up three of the seven 100-loss seasons in Sox history, right in a row. A fourth wouldn’t exactly be shocking, but it would be utterly indefensible.</p><p>Because there’s what progress looks like as Getz and Venable describe it, and then there’s progress in the only way that matters to most people — winning a bunch more games. A major league season is often described by those on the inside as a “marathon,” but it’s Sox fans who know better than anybody what that actually feels like. They’re the ones who’ve been stranded by the side of the road, doubled over and gasping for breaths of any sort of optimism that might power them through another full journey.</p><p>Why is it so hard to put a number on progress?</p><p>Here, I’ll start: The Sox were 28-37 (.431) after the All-Star break last season. Over 162, that translates to right around 70 wins. Boom, there’s your number. Did you see how easy that was?</p><p>Fortunately, there were others willing to play along somewhat.</p><p>Sox radio analyst Darrin Jackson, cornered outside an elevator, mused that after the Sox improved by 19 wins — from 41 to 60 — last season, it would be perfectly A-OK if they were to improve by 19 again. That makes Jackson’s number a relatively robust 79.</p><p>“That’s wishful thinking, though,” he made clear. “Definitely not a prediction.”</p><p>It was demanded of TV analyst Steve Stone, Chicago’s favorite psychic, that he put his mystical powers to the test. He answered that the projected Sox win totals out there on metrics sites and betting sites — in the neighborhood of 66 or 67 — are easily too low.</p><p>“This team has the ability to finish well above that number,” Stone said, putting himself on the record.</p><p>A few hours before homering off Brewers All-Star Jacob Misiorowski in the Sox’ very first at-bat of the season, second baseman Chase Meidroth did more than put a number on it.</p><p>“Making the playoffs, that’s what we’re aiming for,” he said.</p><p>I had to ask if he meant it or was just pulling my leg.</p><p>“We’re going to compete our asses off,” he said. “If you do that over the course of a season, you’re going to look up and be where you want to be.”</p><p>The Sox have some interesting players, for sure. They could do worse than Colson Montgomery, Kyle Teel, Miguel Vargas, Meidroth — and have. Japanese slugger Munetaka Murakami has arrived, clubhouse bidets and all, and it’s honest-to-goodness exciting, especially after he ripped his first home run in the ninth inning Thursday. There are strong prospects in the pipeline, some of whom will show their mugs at Rate Field this year. Why not take a sizable step forward?</p><p>There have been many teams that fared so much better than expected, it defied reason. The 2002 A’s on a bargain-basement budget. The 2003 Marlins, whom Cubs fans might remember. Joe Maddon’s 2008 Rays went from 96 losses to 97 wins and the World Series with the lowest payroll in baseball. The 2024 Brewers were projected to have a losing record and won their division by 10 games. Last year’s Blue Jays were projected to have a losing record and reached Game 7 of the World Series. Crazy stories sometimes unfold.</p><p>What do Getz and Venable really think? It would be nice to know.</p><p>Whether you think you can or think you can’t, you’re right, as the saying goes.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Munetaka Murakami's first MLB homer is a ninth inning blast to cut the White Sox deficit to 12 runs <a href="https://t.co/IHTl6mDIFs">pic.twitter.com/IHTl6mDIFs</a></p>— Talkin' Baseball (@TalkinBaseball_) <a href="https://twitter.com/TalkinBaseball_/status/2037276982082220066?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 26, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p></p>
entries.author: Steve Greenberg

entries.title: Ex-federal prosecutors in Chicago taking on Trump's 'troubling' DOJ: ‘We feel compelled to speak and act'
entries.summary: <p>Fearing President Donald Trump’s Department of Justice<b> </b>is “ignoring its founding principles,”<b> </b>a prominent group of former Chicago federal prosecutors invited<b> </b>more than 300<b> </b>fellow alums<b> </b>this week to join them in moving “from handwringing to action”<b> </b>to preserve the agency’s integrity.</p><p>The 14 signatories on the “Call to Action” email include three former Chicago U.S. attorneys: Dan Webb, Scott Lassar and Patrick Fitzgerald. They're calling for a non-partisan effort led by former department officials acting as “private citizens," according to a copy obtained by the Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>“As lawyers who cherish the role DOJ should play in our criminal justice system, we feel compelled to speak and act against the troubling events underway at the Department,” the email states, “and we invite you to participate in those efforts.”</p><p>It appears to be the first Chicago-specific group of its kind, formed to push back against Trump’s Justice Department. But the group’s email acknowledges that “many like-minded non-partisan organizations”<b> </b>already exist that could serve as partners.</p><p>Their efforts could involve litigation aimed at protecting the “integrity of elections” and a push to ensure federal prosecutors can still be held accountable for misconduct, according to the email.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >Remaining Broadview 6 defendants want records showing Trump administration’s influence on conspiracy case</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The Justice Department did not immediately respond to a message from the Sun-Times seeking comment. But current U.S. Attorney Andrew Boutros said in a statement that his office is "busy doing the people's business, which means prosecuting criminals who commit serious crimes."</p><p>"We recognize the immense power we wield over life, liberty, and reputation, and therefore, we strive every day to exercise that enormous responsibility with integrity, fairness, decency, and humility," Boutros said. "We are not interested in anything that distracts us from our mission."</p><p>The email follows more than a year of Justice Department controversies, including claims that Trump has used the agency to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/13/chicago-trump-abughazaleh-justice-department-broadview-midway-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >retaliate against political foes</a> like ex-FBI Director James Comey.</p><p>Fitzgerald represented Comey<b> </b>in court after Trump’s Justice Department secured an indictment<b> </b>against the former FBI boss. That indictment, in the Eastern District of Virginia, was tossed after a judge found the top prosecutor there acted as U.S. attorney without authorization.</p><p>Former federal prosecutor Ron Safer sent this week’s email and is among its 14 authors. They describe themselves as “Republicans, Independents, Democrats and everything in between.” Webb, Lassar and Fitzgerald were nominated to be U.S. attorney by Presidents Ronald Reagan, Bill Clinton and George W. Bush, respectively.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatLeft> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-440000" name="image-440000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former federal prosecutor Ron Safer in 2018 during a trial at the Leighton Criminal Court Building.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times files</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The other signatories include Sergio<b> </b>Acosta, Sean<b> </b>Berkowitz, Patrick Collins, John Gallo, Kathy<b> </b>Murdock, Lawrence Oliver, Andrew Porter, Julie Porter, Mark Rotert and Andrea Zopp.</p><p>Acosta, Collins, Gallo,<b> </b>Lassar and Safer confirmed their involvement but declined to comment further. The other signatories either did not respond to messages or could not be reached for comment.</p><p>Former President Joe Biden once considered Acosta as a finalist to be Chicago’s U.S. attorney. Berkowitz<b> </b>prosecuted<b> </b>top Enron executives in the 2000s. Collins<b> </b>helped put ex-Gov. George Ryan in prison. And as a defense attorney, Gallo once represented former U.S. House Speaker Dennis Hastert. Zopp is a former head of the Chicago Urban League.</p><p>Former federal prosecutors have pushed back in myriad ways against the direction of the Justice Department since Trump’s return to office 14 months ago. Several <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-corruption-trials/2025/11/10/superstar-prosecutor-convicted-madigan-burke-leaving-u-s-attorneys-office" target="_blank" >left the U.S. attorney’s office</a> in Chicago, including most criminal section chiefs. Others have played prominent roles defending criminal defendants or in litigation against government agencies.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-corruption-trials/2025/11/10/superstar-prosecutor-convicted-madigan-burke-leaving-u-s-attorneys-office" target="_blank" >‘Superstar’ prosecutor who helped bring down Madigan, Burke parting ways with U.S. attorney’s office</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The new Chicago effort is also reminiscent of the <a class="Link" href="https://federaloverreach.org" target="_blank" >Fight Against Federal Overreach</a>, a national coalition of state court prosecutors who say they’ve come together “to ensure federal officials are held accountable when they exceed their lawful authority.”</p><p>But the authors of the Chicago email simply describe themselves as “a very informal group of former colleagues … who have chatted about whether we could move from handwringing to action.”<b> </b>They’re now at law firms of various sizes, and some are retired.</p><p>They say they’ve done “preliminary research”<b> </b>into three specific ways the group might push back against the Justice Department. One involves litigation revolving around what the group called “core issues.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-floatRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-170000" name="image-170000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
